headline: Feel Like Yourself Again
body:
My husband crawled out of a customer's house on his hands and knees last March.

He thought I didn't know. He pulled into the driveway, sat in his truck for 11 minutes, then came inside and told me he was tired and going to lay down. He didn't eat dinner. He didn't take his boots off. He just walked past me in the kitchen and went straight to the bedroom.

The customer called me the next morning. A woman named Diane on the east side of Toledo. She found my number on the business card he'd left on her kitchen counter. She didn't want to make trouble. She just wanted to make sure he was okay. She said he'd gone under her sink to fix a leaking trap and when he tried to stand back up he couldn't. His legs gave out. His face went white. He sat on her kitchen floor for 20 minutes before he could get himself up, and then he braced against the counter and walked out to his truck like a man who'd been shot.

She watched him from the front window. He sat in his truck for a long time before he drove away. She said she'd never been so worried about a stranger in her life.

I thanked her for calling. I hung up the phone. I stood in our kitchen and stared at the coffee maker for I don't know how long.

Then I walked down the hall and I sat on the edge of our bed and I looked at my husband sleeping with his work clothes still on, and I started planning what I was going to do.

I'm 57 years old. My name is Cheryl. I live in Toledo, Ohio with my husband, Rick, who is 61. We've been married 34 years this October. We met at a Bob Evans on Reynolds Road in 1989 when I was waiting tables and he came in with two of his buddies after a job. He had grease under his fingernails and he asked for extra ketchup and he tipped me 4 dollars on a 7 dollar check, which in 1989 was a fortune.

Rick has been a plumber since he was 19. His dad was a plumber. His granddad was a plumber. He started his own company in 1998 and named it after our daughter, Hailey, who is 32 now and lives in Cincinnati with her two boys. The truck still says Hailey's Plumbing on the side, even though the logo is faded and the H is starting to peel.

He works alone. He's always worked alone. He had a guy work with him for a couple of years back in 2011 but it didn't take, and after that he said he'd rather just do it himself. He likes the quiet. He likes being his own boss. He likes that when a customer asks him a question, he's the one who knows the answer.

He is the most stubborn man God ever made.

And his blood pressure had been climbing for 4 years.

The first time the doctor mentioned it was 2021. Rick had gone in for a physical because I made him. His pressure was 148 over 94. The doctor said it was elevated and he wanted to see it again in 3 months.

Rick didn't go back in 3 months. He went back the next year. It was 152 over 96.

The doctor put him on a low dose lisinopril. Rick took it for about 6 weeks and then quit because he said it made him cough.

I begged him to call the doctor and get a different one. He said he'd think about it. He didn't think about it.

The next year his pressure was 159 over 98. The doctor sat him down and told him in plain language that if he didn't get this under control he was going to have a stroke. Rick is a man who only respects plain language. He took the prescription. This time it was a different medication. He took it for 2 months. Then he forgot one day. Then he forgot two days. Then he forgot a week. Then the bottle sat in our medicine cabinet and I'd see it every time I brushed my teeth.

He won't talk about it. I have tried. I have tried in the kitchen. I have tried in the car. I have tried in bed at night with the lights off. I have tried writing it in a card on his birthday. I have tried bringing it up casually while we're watching the Tigers play.

He always says the same thing. "I'm fine, Cheryl. I'll go in when I need to."

Last winter I started noticing things. He'd sit at the kitchen table after work and put his head in his hands for a long minute before he got up to take his boots off. He'd come out of the bathroom in the morning and grip the doorframe before he started walking. He'd be carrying a tool box to the truck and he'd set it down on the porch step and just stand there.

I started watching him without him knowing I was watching him.

His face was a color I didn't have a word for. Not red. Not flushed. Something deeper. Like the blood was sitting under his skin in a place it wasn't supposed to be.

In February I went into his truck while he was in the shower one morning. I was looking for a Costco card I thought he'd left in the console. I opened the glove box and I found a blood pressure cuff. The kind you get at CVS. It was still in its box, but the box was open and the cuff had been used.

I took the cuff out. I looked at the digital screen. The last reading still on it was from 4 days earlier.

171 over 103.

I sat in that truck in our driveway in my bathrobe and I cried. Big silent crying. Don't want to wake the neighbors crying.

171 over 103 is the number where doctors stop being patient with you. 171 over 103 is the number where you sit in a hospital waiting room. 171 over 103 is the number where your wife gets a phone call she can't unhear.

He was checking it. He knew. He knew the whole time. He just wasn't telling me.

I put the cuff back in the glove box. I closed the door quietly. I walked back inside and I made him eggs.

That was 4 weeks before Diane called me about the kitchen floor.

When I got off that call with Diane I knew I couldn't keep doing what I'd been doing. I'd been begging. I'd been hinting. I'd been leaving articles printed off the internet on his side of the bed. He'd been laughing at me and saying I worried too much.

I needed something different.

I called my sister Patty.

Patty is 3 years younger than me. She lives in Sylvania. She's a retired school nurse from Sylvania Northview. She's the kind of person who knows things. Not because she's a doctor, but because she has spent 30 years dealing with parents who didn't know what to do with their kids' problems and she figured out, somewhere along the way, that most of the answers are not where most people are looking.

I told her everything. Diane. The truck. The cuff. The number. The bottles in the medicine cabinet that he wasn't taking.

She was quiet for a long time.

Then she said, "Cheryl. I want to tell you about something I've been doing. I wasn't going to bring it up because I didn't know if it was for you. But I think you need to hear about it now."

She told me about a thing called amla.

I'd never heard the word. I asked her to spell it. A. M. L. A.

She said it's a kind of berry. From India. The Indian gooseberry. People have been using it over there for thousands of years. She started taking it about a year and a half ago because her own pressure was creeping up after her hysterectomy. Her doctor wanted to put her on medication. She wanted to see if she could try something else first. She did her research. She talked to her son in law's mother, who is from Mumbai. She landed on amla.

She'd been taking it for 18 months. Her pressure had come down on its own. Her last reading was 124 over 78. Her doctor was confused. She didn't care.

I said, "Patty. Rick is not going to take a berry from India because his sister in law told him to."

She laughed. She said, "I know. I am not asking you to ask him. I am asking you to learn about it. Learn about it. Then decide what to do."

I spent the next 2 weeks on my laptop at the kitchen table after Rick fell asleep at night. I read until 1 in the morning some nights. I had a notebook. I had pages and pages of notes. I made Patty answer the phone twice a day because I had more questions every time.

What I started to understand was that blood pressure isn't just about the number. The number is the smoke. The fire is somewhere else.

The fire is in the blood vessels themselves. The lining of them. There's this thin layer on the inside of every artery in your body called the endothelium. When that lining is healthy, it's smooth and flexible and it tells your blood vessels when to relax and when to contract. When it's damaged, the whole system gets stiff. The vessels don't open the way they should. The heart has to push harder to move the same amount of blood. The pressure goes up.

What damages the endothelium? Oxidative stress. Inflammation. The slow erosion that happens to all of us over the years, but happens faster in some people than in others. Specifically the kind of people who are stubborn 61 year old plumbers who eat at gas stations 4 days a week and crawl under sinks all day and don't slow down when their bodies tell them to.

The medication Rick had been refusing to take works by forcing the system. It's like a man pushing on the gas pedal of a car with a stuck brake. It works, but it doesn't fix the brake.

Amla, what I learned, works at the source. It's one of the most antioxidant rich foods in the world. It contains compounds that have been associated with supporting healthy nitric oxide production, which is what tells your blood vessels to relax. It's been linked to helping maintain healthy endothelial function and supporting the body's natural ability to manage oxidative stress.

In plain language, the kind of language Rick would respect, it gives the body the materials it needs to do its own job. It doesn't override the system. It supports the system. Like sending a guy a better wrench instead of yelling at him for being slow.

I read about a specific brand called Alevia. Their product is just called Alevia Amla. Small dark glass bottle. Two capsules a day. They source their amla from a specific traditional growing region in India and they don't cut it with fillers or fake stuff like a lot of supplement brands do. They have a 90 day money back guarantee, which mattered to me because I knew if I bought it and it didn't work for him I'd have to eat the cost without telling him.

I ordered a bottle. I had it shipped to Patty's house because Rick checks the mail.

When it came in I drove to Sylvania and picked it up and I drove home and I sat with it on the kitchen table for an hour trying to figure out how I was going to do this.

I decided I wasn't going to ask. I wasn't going to explain. I was going to put it next to his coffee mug in the morning with a glass of water, and I was going to tell him my sister Patty had given it to me and she'd asked me to try it for my own circulation, and I'd been taking it and feeling better, and I'd put one out for him just in case.

I laid it like a trap. A loving trap.

He came downstairs at 6:15 in the morning. Sat at the table. Picked up the capsules. Turned them over. Looked at me.

He said, "What is this?"

I said, "Patty's been on it. She says it's good for circulation. I started taking it last week. I put one out for you in case you wanted to try it."

He said, "Cheryl."

I said, "Rick."

He looked at me for a long minute. Then he picked them up and he swallowed them with his coffee.

He didn't say anything else about it. He just took them.

He took them the next morning too. And the morning after that.

Within 2 weeks I noticed something. He was getting out of bed in the morning and not gripping the doorframe. I didn't say anything. I just noticed.

Week 3, he came home from work and instead of putting his head in his hands at the kitchen table he sat down and asked me how my day was.

Week 4, he brought up the cuff. Out of nowhere. We were eating dinner. He said, "I checked my pressure this morning."

I tried to keep my face still. I said, "Oh yeah?"

He said, "It was 142 over 89."

I said, "Rick. That's lower than the doctor's number from last year."

He said, "I know."

He didn't say anything else for a few minutes. Then he said, "I'm going to keep checking it."

That was the first time he'd ever told me his number on his own. In 4 years of refusing to talk about it, he had just volunteered the information.

Week 6, he came home from work with a brown paper bag. He set it on the kitchen counter. He pulled out a head of cauliflower and some bell peppers and some chicken breasts.

I said, "What's this?"

He said, "Diane told me to start eating better."

I said, "Diane?"

He said, "The lady on the east side. With the leaking trap. I went back today to fix the second valve. She made me sit down and have a glass of water before I started. She said her husband died of a stroke when he was 64 and she didn't want to read about another plumber in the obituaries."

I started crying at the counter and he pretended not to notice while he put the chicken in the fridge.

Week 8, he was sleeping better. He hadn't snored that loud, gasping kind of snore in over a month. He was waking up before the alarm. He was making me coffee instead of the other way around.

Week 10, he went to his doctor.

I drove him because I wanted to be there. I sat in the waiting room. He came out with a piece of paper.

136 over 84.

His doctor said it was the best reading he'd had in 5 years and he didn't know what Rick was doing different but he should keep doing it.

Rick told the doctor he was eating cauliflower. He didn't mention the amla. I almost laughed in the waiting room.

We sat in the truck in the parking lot after. He looked at the paper for a long time.

He said, "Cheryl. I almost died on that kitchen floor in March."

I said, "I know."

He said, "How did you know?"

I said, "Diane called me."

He didn't say anything for a long time. Then he said, "I owe that woman a lot more than a fixed valve."

We drove home in silence. He held my hand across the truck console. He hadn't done that since we were dating.

It's been 11 months now. His pressure is steady in the low 130s over high 70s. He still works full time. He still crawls under sinks. But he stands up slowly. He drinks more water than he used to. He brings me a glass of water without me asking.

He takes the amla every morning. He doesn't make a big deal out of it. He just takes it.

About 3 months ago he told one of his buddies about it. The guy is another plumber named Gary who's about Rick's age and has been on blood pressure medication for 9 years. Rick told him about the amla. Just casually. Like he was recommending a tool. Gary ordered some. Now Gary's wife calls me once a week to tell me Gary is doing better than he has in years.

I'm telling you this story because I know what it's like to watch a man you love refuse to take care of himself. I know what it's like to find a blood pressure cuff hidden in a truck. I know what it's like to get a phone call from a stranger telling you your husband couldn't stand up.

I know how alone you feel when you're the only one in the house who's worried.

If you're reading this and any part of it sounds familiar, I want you to look into Alevia Amla. Not as a replacement for what your doctor is telling you to do. Just as something that supports the body the way the body was supposed to be supported all along.

The 90 day money back guarantee is what got me to try it. I bought it knowing if it didn't work I could send it back and I wouldn't have lost anything but the gas money to Patty's house. They mean the guarantee. I've never had to use it but a friend of Patty's did and she got her money back without an argument.

The bottle is small. Two capsules a day. It comes in the mail and you take it with your coffee. That's the whole thing.

I almost lost the man I met at Bob Evans in 1989. I came so close I can still feel it in my chest.

If your husband, or your father, or your brother, or you, is being stubborn about this, please. Please. Do what I did. Stop begging. Start learning. And then figure out the loving trap that gets the people you love to do the thing they won't do for themselves.

Diane is coming over for dinner next month. Rick invited her himself. He said he wanted me to meet the woman who saved his life. I told him she didn't save his life. I did. He laughed and said we'd just have to agree to disagree.

I think we're both right.

P.S. The bottle really is small. Don't let that throw you. When mine first came in the mail Rick lifted it and said, "This is the whole thing?" That's amla. It's potent. You don't need a tub of powder. You need two capsules.

P.P.S. The 90 day guarantee is the only reason I had the nerve to try this. I was 4 years deep into worrying about my husband and I couldn't afford another disappointment. Knowing I could send it back if it didn't work was the only thing that let me click order. They keep their word. If you try it and it doesn't help, you send it back. That's the deal.

P.P.P.S. If you're the one with the high pressure and you're reading this thinking your wife or your husband is overreacting, I want you to know what you look like from the outside. You look like a person they love who they're watching disappear in slow motion. They are not nagging you. They are scared. Let them love you. Take the capsule. It's two capsules. It's not a betrayal of your stubbornness. It's a gift to the person who has to watch you.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
A woman whose garden plot has been next to mine for three years gave me back my Saturdays. 

My name is Nancy.

I am sixty-four.

I live with my husband outside Madison, Wisconsin, in the same small house we have been in for thirty-three years.

We have a son in Milwaukee and a daughter in Minneapolis.

I retired three years ago, after twenty-seven years as a property manager at a small real estate firm two blocks from our downtown.

I managed about a hundred and forty small rental properties.

I knew the families. I knew which furnaces were old. I knew which roofs we were going to be on the phone about that winter.

It was steady work for a careful woman.

I am, on paper, in fine shape.

I am on no prescription medications.

I take a multivitamin and a vitamin D and that is the entirety of my pill organizer.

My doctor — Dr. Stewart, a careful woman I have been seeing for thirteen years — tells me at every annual visit that my bloodwork is in the ranges she wants it in.

I walk every morning.

I eat the way a woman my age should eat.

I sleep seven hours a night.

That is the part I want you to hold while you read this.

I want to tell you, plainly, about a quiet question I had been carrying for almost two years.

It was small.

It would not have registered on any chart.

I am not telling you a dramatic story.

I am telling you what one careful woman in her sixties had been noticing quietly about herself, alone, for almost two years, before another woman said one sentence to her on a bench at the community garden in May.

It looked like this.

I have a small plot at our town community garden.

I have been gardening there for five years.

The plot is twenty feet by ten.

I grow tomatoes, peppers, basil, parsley, and a row of beans.

I had been planting it every May in two long Saturday mornings for five years.

For the previous two springs, the same two Saturday mornings of planting had become four Saturday mornings.

I had been needing to come home in the middle and lie down.

I had been needing to leave half the work for Sunday morning, and to come back on Monday for the parts I had not finished.

I had been telling myself it was the heat.

It was not the heat.

I had been telling myself it was the new mulch I had been using, which was heavier than the old one.

It was not the new mulch.

I had been telling myself a great many things.

It looked like the afternoon.

I had been a woman who never took an afternoon nap.

I had not been a napper at fifty. I had not been a napper at fifty-five. I had not been a napper at sixty.

Somewhere in the second year of my retirement, I had started lying down on the couch after lunch.

I had told myself it was retirement.

It was not retirement.

It was the quiet pull of an afternoon I had not felt before, that started somewhere just past sixty-two, and that I had been giving in to once a week, then twice a week, then most days.

It looked like my book club.

I am in a book club that meets once a month at our library on Tuesday evenings.

I had been a four-books-a-month woman for forty years.

We read one for the club. I read three others on my own.

For the previous eighteen months I had been falling asleep on the couch after dinner with the book club book open on my lap.

I had been reading the same paragraph over.

I had been finishing the book club book on Tuesday at five in the afternoon, in a rush, because I had not finished reading it at the natural pace I had used to read at for forty years.

I had not finished three other books in eight months.

I went into Dr. Stewart's office for my annual physical last February.

She did the bloodwork.

Everything was in the ranges she wanted it in.

Not borderline. Not perfect. In the ranges.

She looked at me across her desk.

She said: anything we should be talking about, Nancy?

I told her about the garden.

I told her about the afternoons.

I told her about the book club books.

I told her I was not asking her for an answer. I just wanted her to have the picture.

She listened.

She wrote it down.

She said: I do not see anything on your chart I would do anything about today. But you know your body better than I do. If something changes, or if you want to come back sooner, you call me.

I told her thank you.

I drove home thinking about that conversation.

What I drove home thinking was this.

Dr. Stewart was not wrong.

I was not wrong either.

There is a kind of quiet shift in your sixties that does not show up on the chart.

It is not a thing to panic about.

It is not a thing to ignore.

It is, I decided that afternoon, a thing to pay attention to.

I had been carrying it quietly for two years before I told Dr. Stewart about it.

I had been carrying it quietly for two more weeks before I sat on a bench at the community garden.

The community garden is two blocks from my house.

It has thirty-two plots.

The woman whose plot is next to mine is named Sandy.

She is, I would guess, sixty-seven.

Silver hair, a quiet manner, gardens in slow careful rows the way a woman who has been gardening for a long time gardens.

We had been nodding at each other for three years.

We had said hello and goodbye and complimented each other's tomatoes and asked each other about the rain, the way two women who garden next to each other for three years do.

That kind of friendship is its own thing.

You can have it with another woman for three years at a community garden without ever really knowing very much about her.

The Saturday in May was a Saturday like every other Saturday.

I had been at my plot since seven, planting the last row of tomatoes.

I was tired.

I had been planning to plant the peppers, too, and I knew I was not going to make it.

I went and sat on the long bench at the edge of the garden area.

It was eight-thirty.

I had been planting for an hour and a half.

I was already done for the morning.

I was already going to come back on Sunday.

I sat on the bench with my water bottle.

I was angry at my body.

I had been angry at my body off and on for two years.

Sandy came over.

She had been at her own plot.

She sat down on the bench next to me.

She did not say anything for a long time.

She just sat there with her own water bottle.

Then she said: Nancy, I have been meaning to ask you. Are you alright? You have been on this bench more often this spring than I remember from the other springs.

I sat with that for a moment.

I looked at my hands.

I said: I am not unwell. My doctor is happy with me. The numbers are fine. The chart is fine. I am just tired in a way I was not tired three years ago. The garden tires me out. The afternoons tire me out. The books tire me out. I do not know how to say it any plainer.

Sandy was quiet for a long minute.

Then she said something I have been thinking about every day since.

She said: Nancy. I had been quietly noticing the same thing about myself for almost three years before I decided to do anything about it. I had been sitting on this bench more than I had been planting, and I had been telling myself it was sixty-four. It was not sixty-four. It was something quieter than that.

She told me she had been noticing her own shifts since her early sixties.

Slow morning routine.

The afternoon pull.

Reading less.

She told me she had told herself it was retirement. She told me she had told herself it was the dog. She told me she had told herself a great many things.

Then she said: about four years ago, I added one small thing to my own mornings. I read for a month before I added it. I talked to my doctor and to my pharmacist before I added it. I want to be careful with you about what I say next, because I am one woman and you are looking at one woman and bodies are different.

But I have been more like myself for the last four years than I was for the three years before that. I have been gardening more steadily. I have been finishing my book club books on time. I have been getting through a full Saturday without lying down. I would not tell you it is the beetroot capsule on its own. I would tell you it is one thing I added to everything else.

She said it without performance.

She said it the way a woman in her late sixties says something to another woman on a bench at the community garden where they have been gardening next to each other for three years.

She wasn't selling me anything.

She was just telling me.

I asked her what she had added.

She said it was a beetroot capsule.

She said the brand was called Rosabella.

She said she had picked it because the small company that made it cold-pressed the beetroot at low temperatures rather than processing it with heat, and because her doctor had specifically said how the supplement was processed mattered for what would make it from the field into the capsule.

The morning was getting hot.

She stood up.

She put her hand on my shoulder before she walked back to her plot.

She said: Nancy. Please call your pharmacist before you add anything. That is the only thing I am going to be insistent about. Promise me.

I promised her.

She walked back to her plot.

I sat on the bench for another twenty minutes.

I drove home thinking about Sandy.

I drove the rest of that week thinking about Sandy.

By the weekend, I was thinking about what I was going to do.

I am, by temperament, a careful woman.

A property manager for twenty-seven years is, almost by definition, a careful woman.

I do not add things to my body without understanding what I am adding.

I went home and I started reading.

I read for almost three weeks.

I read about beetroot.

I read about the deep crimson pigment in the root.

It is called betalain.

It is the color that stains your cutting board for three days.

It is also, according to a long and patient body of research the cardiovascular community has been quietly building for decades, one of the more potent antioxidants in the food supply.

I read about the long, slowly built relationship the structure-function research has had with this vegetable.

In the context of supporting healthy circulation.

Supporting cardiovascular wellness.

Supporting the body's own cellular energy.

In people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

That last one is the one I had been waiting to find.

Cellular energy.

It is the basement-level work the body does to keep doing its work.

It is not the same thing as energy in a coffee cup.

It is not the same thing as the energy you get from a candy bar at four in the afternoon.

It is the quiet, steady work the body has to do, all day every day, just to keep being the body it has been being.

In people my age, in good health, who are doing all the careful things, that quiet work is something the research community talks about supporting.

Not boosting. Not fixing. Not treating.

Supporting.

In a structure-function way.

For people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

That was the shape of the language I had been hoping to find.

I read about the cold-press, too.

The chemistry is straightforward.

The betalain pigment is heat-sensitive.

If you process beetroot the standard way, with heat, you lose some of the compound to the heat.

Rosabella cold-presses at low temperatures because they want as much of the compound as possible to make it from the field into the capsule.

That is a slower way to make a supplement.

It costs them more.

At sixty-four, after twenty-seven years of watching small landlords in our town make small decisions about their own properties, I have come to pay close attention to the small decisions companies make when no one is watching.

Those decisions tell you everything.

The other thing I liked was the simplicity.

Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water.

That is the whole protocol.

I have a husband, a garden plot, a book club, and a couch.

I am not interested in a protocol that requires its own shelf.

Now I want to tell you the most important part of this letter.

I made Sandy a promise at the garden.

I kept it.

Before I added anything to what I was already taking, I called my pharmacist.

His name is William.

He has been the pharmacist at the small independent pharmacy three blocks from our house for sixteen years.

I have been filling things there for as long as we have lived in town.

I called him for a specific reason.

I am on no prescription medications.

I take a multivitamin and a vitamin D.

I called William anyway.

I called because I am a careful woman and because I wanted somebody who knew what was on my record to confirm that adding a beetroot capsule to a multivitamin was the right thing to do at my age.

He pulled up my profile.

He confirmed there were no concerns at the dosage on the label.

He told me to take it in the morning with my coffee the way the bottle suggested.

He told me to call him back if Dr. Stewart ever started me on any prescription medication in the future, because the conversation would be different at that point.

He told me, before he got off the phone, that he wished more of his patients made the call I had just made.

I also called Dr. Stewart's office that week.

I left a message with her nurse, with the same information.

She called me back the next day to tell me Dr. Stewart had no concerns about my adding it at the dosage on the label.

I want to say something now, plainly, to every reader of this letter.

If you are on any prescription medication, especially anything cardiovascular, please make the call before you add anything new.

I made two calls.

I would have made three if I had been on a prescription.

So should you.

The two minutes the call takes is the most important two minutes of your engagement with any supplement.

I started on a Monday in early June.

I set the bottle on the counter next to the coffee maker.

I took the two capsules with my first sip of coffee.

The first two weeks I felt nothing in particular.

I had read enough alongside what Sandy had told me to know that bodies are different.

Some women notice something within the first week or two.

Some women take much longer.

Some women don't notice much at all.

I was prepared to land in any of those camps.

I was prepared to be honest with myself about the result.

What I noticed, I noticed slowly.

The first thing I noticed, around week six, was the garden.

It was a Saturday morning in mid-July.

I had been at my plot since seven.

By nine-thirty I had planted the second wave of beans, weeded both rows of tomatoes, and watered the basil that the heat had been hard on.

I had not been to the bench.

I came home at ten-fifteen.

I made myself a sandwich for lunch.

I did not lie down on the couch.

I sat on the back porch with a book for an hour.

I did not say anything about it to my husband.

I did not want to make a thing of it.

I had read enough not to make a thing of one observation.

The second thing I noticed, around week nine, was a book.

It was a Wednesday evening.

I was on the couch after dinner.

I was on the second chapter of the book club book.

I read the second chapter.

I read the third.

I read the fourth.

I looked at the clock and it was ten-fifteen.

I had been reading for an hour and a half without falling asleep, without re-reading the same paragraph, without putting the book down on my chest.

I finished the book by Friday.

I finished the next book I picked up by the following Tuesday.

I read four books that month.

The third thing I noticed was Sunday dinner.

My son and daughter-in-law and their two children come down from Milwaukee for Sunday dinner once a month.

I had been hosting them for sixteen years.

For the last two years, I had been needing to lie down on the couch for an hour before they arrived, just to get through the visit.

The Sunday in early October was the first one after I had been on Rosabella for four months.

I made a roast chicken, mashed potatoes, salad, and a small pumpkin pie.

I set the table.

They arrived at one.

I hosted them for three hours.

I cleaned up the kitchen with my daughter-in-law afterward.

We sat in the living room for another hour after the kids went outside to play in our yard.

I went to bed at the normal time that night.

I had not needed to lie down at any point during the day.

I sat in the kitchen at the end of that Sunday and I drank a small glass of water by myself and I let myself notice it.

I want to be careful with what I am telling you.

I am not telling you Rosabella did any of those things on its own.

I had been walking a little more deliberately after my conversation with Sandy.

I had been going to bed at the same time every night.

I had been doing the careful things I had been doing for forty years, just a little more carefully.

Something had unlocked in me the morning I sat on that bench with Sandy that had nothing to do with a capsule on a saucer.

There were several things changing at once, the way there are when a careful woman finally tells another woman the thing she has been carrying quietly for two years.

All I am telling you is that I added one small thing to a long list of things I was newly doing.

I kept everything else exactly the same.

And a stretch of months followed that was qualitatively different from the two years that had preceded it.

That is the honest version.

I would not give you a cleaner one.

I went back to see Dr. Stewart in November for a follow-up.

She did not have to repeat the bloodwork.

She looked at me.

She said: you look more rested than you did in February.

I told her about the garden, the books, the Sunday dinners.

I told her about the beetroot.

I told her I had called William before I started.

She nodded.

She said: that was the right way to go about it. The research on antioxidant support is good. Keep doing what you are doing, and come back at your usual time.

I drove home from her office that afternoon.

I had been driving home from Dr. Stewart's office for thirteen years.

This was the first time I had driven home thinking that I felt like the woman who had been driving home from that office five years ago.

I am telling you all of this because I think you might be where I was the morning I sat on that bench.

Sitting somewhere quietly, at sixty-three or sixty-four or sixty-five, in fine shape on paper, tired in a way the chart does not name.

If you are a woman in your sixties who has been telling yourself it is retirement, or sixty-four, or just aging, I want to say a few things plainly.

I do not know you.

I do not know your bloodwork, your medications, or what your doctor has told you.

Bodies are different.

Some women notice something within the first week or two of adding something like this.

Some women take much longer.

Some women don't notice much at all.

That is the honest version.

Anyone offering you a cleaner one is selling you harder than I am.

Here is how I think about Rosabella now, in the words I would actually use.

It is something the body uses.

The research community talks about it in the context of supporting healthy circulation, supporting cardiovascular wellness, and supporting the body's own cellular energy.

In people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

It is not a fix for anything.

It is not a substitute for a medication.

It is not a treatment for fatigue.

It is not a treatment for the quiet decline a woman can have in her sixties.

It is not a treatment for tiredness, or for aging, or for any of it.

It is one small thing you add to the long list of things you are already doing for yourself, alongside everything else.

Please call your pharmacist or your doctor's office before you add it.

If you are on any blood pressure medication, cholesterol medication, or anything cardiovascular at all, this is non-negotiable.

I called William.

I called Dr. Stewart's office.

I would not have added anything to a prescription without those calls.

Neither should you.

The people who know your chart will tell you in two minutes whether the supplement is safe for you to add given what you are already taking.

I am a sixty-four-year-old retired property manager in Wisconsin.

I am not the person to ask.

Your pharmacist or your doctor is.

The company has a ninety-day satisfaction guarantee.

If you are not satisfied — for any reason at all, including just changing your mind — they refund you.

It is not tied to anything you have to prove.

If you are not satisfied, you are not satisfied.

That is the whole policy.

The beets are grown on a small operation that harvests on its own schedule.

There are stretches when they are in stock and stretches when they are not.

I mention that not to push you — please do not let anybody push you into a health decision — but so that if you go to the site and they are out, you know why.

That is all I have.

Thank you for reading this far.

I hope something here was useful to you.

I hope, somewhere today, your own Sandy is sitting on a bench at your own community garden, waiting for you to come sit next to her with your water bottle.

— Nancy

P.S. I want to come back to the cold-press for a second.

The betalain pigment — the deep crimson, the part the research community keeps circling back to — is heat-sensitive.

That is the chemistry of the molecule.

Process beetroot the standard way, with heat, and you lose some of the compound to the heat.

Rosabella chose the slow way.

Cold-press. Low temperatures. More of the compound through to the capsule.

It costs them money. It costs them time.

They made the choice anyway.

At sixty-four, after twenty-seven years of paying attention to small operations in my own town, I have come to believe that the small decisions companies make when no one is watching tell you everything.

P.P.S. Two capsules. In the morning. With a glass of water.

That is the entire protocol.

No powder to mix.

No afternoon dose to remember.

No schedule taped to the inside of the cupboard.

I keep the bottle on the counter next to the coffee maker.

I take the capsules with my first sip.

That has been the rhythm of my morning for almost a year.

A simple morning.

I have earned one.

P.P.P.S. I am going to say it one more time, because I have to.

Please.

Call your pharmacist or your doctor's office before you add anything new to what you are already taking.

Especially if you are on a blood pressure medication, a cholesterol medication, or anything cardiovascular.

They have your whole picture in front of them.

I have written you a letter.

There is a difference, and the difference matters.

The two minutes it takes to ask are the most important two minutes of your engagement with any supplement.

P.P.P.P.S. The guarantee, again, in case you skimmed.

Ninety days. Any reason at all.

You do not have to prove a single thing — not to them, not about your numbers, not about how you feel.

If you tried it and you are not satisfied, you are not satisfied.

They send the money back.

That is the whole policy.

A small-company policy.

The kind of thing companies used to do when companies still answered the phone.

P.P.P.P.P.S. The thing Sandy said to me on the bench — I had been sitting on this bench more than I had been planting, and I had been telling myself it was sixty-four; it was not sixty-four; it was something quieter than that — has been with me every day for almost a year.

I had been doing that exact thing about myself for two years before Sandy said it.

I had not realized, until she said it out loud on a bench, what I had been doing.

I had been telling myself it was retirement.

I had been telling myself it was sixty-four.

I had been telling myself a great many things, the way careful women do, to keep from saying out loud the small quiet thing.

If you are a woman who has been sitting on her own bench longer than she has been planting, I want you to know you are not the only one.

You are not imagining the quiet.

You are not being dramatic.

You are reading your own body, which is what a careful woman in her sixties is supposed to be doing.

I hope you find your own Sandy at your own community garden, or your own bench, or wherever you tend to end up sitting when you need to sit.

I hope you accept the conversation if she offers one.

I hope you read carefully.

I hope you make the call before you add anything.

I hope, in a year from now, you are at your own plot on a Saturday morning, planting the second wave of beans at nine-thirty, with no plan to sit on the bench.

I hope that for you.

That is the whole reason I wrote this.

— N.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
I built things for forty-one years. At sixty-three I retired two years early, because my body had quietly stopped keeping up. 

My name is Charles.

People have called me Chuck since I was a boy.

I am sixty-five.

I live with my wife outside Spokane, Washington, in the small house we have owned for twenty-eight years.

We have two grown sons.

One is in Seattle. One is in Boise.

I have one grandson in Spokane who is nine and plays Little League baseball.

I was a commercial construction supervisor for forty-one years.

I started on a framing crew when I was twenty-two.

I worked my way up.

The last twenty-two years of my career, I was a project supervisor for a regional construction firm — the man who walked the site at six in the morning, made the day's calls, climbed the scaffolding when somebody needed to climb the scaffolding, drove the four hours to the remote sites when a remote site needed someone to drive to it, and locked up at six at night.

I was good at it.

I had planned to do it until I was sixty-five.

I retired at sixty-three.

That is the part I want you to hold while you read this.

I have been on a low-dose blood pressure medication for ten years.

My doctor — Dr. Klein, a careful man I have been seeing for twelve years — put me on it when I was fifty-five.

The numbers had been creeping a little.

He started me on the lowest dose.

I have been on the same low-dose for ten years.

My numbers have been in the ranges Dr. Klein wants them in ever since.

Not borderline. Not perfect. In the ranges.

I had been doing the careful prudent things alongside the medication for ten years.

Walking forty minutes a day with my wife.

Eating the way a man with my numbers should eat.

Sleeping seven hours a night when the job let me.

Not drinking more than one or two beers on a weekend.

I had been a man whose chart said he was fine for ten years.

That is the other part I want you to hold.

The chart was not what told me I had to retire.

What told me I had to retire was three years of quietly slowing down on the job in ways the chart could not see.

I am going to tell you what I mean.

It looked like the scaffolding.

There is a feeling a man gets in his legs and his chest when he climbs a ladder onto scaffolding three stories up with a thirty-pound tool belt around his waist.

For forty years that feeling had been the feeling of a man's body doing what a man's body does.

Somewhere in my late fifties, the feeling started to be the feeling of a man's body asking for a moment.

In the last three years of the job, the feeling had become the feeling of a man's body asking for several moments.

I had been having to stand on the second platform for thirty seconds before I went up to the third.

I had not stood on the second platform for thirty seconds in thirty-eight years.

Nobody had seen me do it.

I had been doing it when nobody was looking.

It looked like the drive.

I had been driving four hours to a remote site every other week for twenty years.

The drive had been four hours of country highway in my truck, with a thermos of coffee and the radio.

For forty years, the drive home at the end of the day had been the drive home.

In the last three years of the job, the drive home had become the hardest part of the day.

I had been pulling into rest areas to put my head against the steering wheel for ten minutes.

I had not been telling my wife.

I had been arriving home an hour later than I had been telling her I would arrive.

I had been telling her there had been traffic.

There had not been traffic.

It looked like five-thirty in the morning.

I had been a five-thirty-a.m. man for forty-one years.

The job started at six.

I had been at the kitchen counter at five-fifteen for forty years.

In the last year of the job, I had been at the kitchen counter at five-fifty.

I had been showering at five-thirty when I had been showering at five for forty years.

I had been getting to the site at six-oh-five when I had been getting there at five-fifty for forty years.

Nobody had said anything about it.

They were not going to say anything about it.

I had been the boss.

I had been losing fifteen minutes off the front of my day.

It looked like the foreman.

We had a young foreman on the last big job of my career.

His name was Eric.

He was thirty-one.

I had hired him out of his community college program five years earlier.

He was good.

He was going to be very good.

I watched him, on the last job, do the things at the end of the day I used to do at the end of the day.

I watched him climb back up onto the third platform at four-fifty in the afternoon to check something he had been thinking about.

I watched him walk a quarter mile of the site one more time before locking up at six.

I watched him with the energy at the end of the day that I had been a man with at thirty-one.

I was a man with no energy at the end of the day at sixty-three.

I sat in my truck in the parking lot on a Friday in October two years ago and I said, out loud, to no one: the job needs more than you have left to give it.

I had been saying that quietly to myself for two years.

I had not let myself say it out loud.

I came home that night.

I sat with my wife at the kitchen table.

I told her.

She had been waiting for me to tell her.

She had been watching me for three years.

She said: Chuck. The job is not going to stop being the job. You have to be the one who steps back.

I called my company on Monday.

We worked out a six-month consulting transition.

I was off the site by the end of April.

I was retired by the end of October.

Two years earlier than I had planned.

I had not been ready.

I had been right anyway.

The first year of retirement was hard.

Not because I missed the work.

I missed the work some, the way a man misses anything he did for forty-one years.

The harder part was that I did not know what to do with the body I had now.

I had retired because the body could not do the work.

Now the body had no work.

I had been planning to do small construction projects in my garage workshop.

Build a bench for the entryway. Build a small shed for a friend. Maybe a workbench for my grandson when he was old enough.

I did not do any of them.

I sat in my recliner more than I had ever sat in a recliner.

I drove my wife crazy.

She did not say so.

She did not have to.

I knew.

I went into Dr. Klein's office for my annual physical in February of last year.

Bloodwork in the ranges.

He looked at me across his desk.

He said: Chuck. You look like a man who is sitting in a chair more than he is used to. How is retirement?

I told him.

I told him about the recliner and the bench I had not built and the year I had been quietly losing alongside the years I had been quietly losing on the job.

He listened.

He told me he was not surprised to hear what I was telling him.

He told me a lot of men in their early sixties who retire from physically demanding work go through what I was going through.

He told me there was nothing on the chart he would do anything about today.

He told me to keep walking, keep eating the way I was eating, keep taking the medication, and to start asking myself, gently, what one small additive thing I might bring back into my mornings that was not the job.

I drove home from his office thinking about that.

I sat with it for a few weeks.

I started reading.

I read for almost three weeks.

I am, by temperament, a careful man.

A man who runs commercial construction sites for twenty-two years does not put anything into his body without understanding what he is putting in.

I read about beetroot.

I read about the deep crimson pigment in the root.

It is called betalain.

It is the color that stains your cutting board for three days.

It is also, according to a long and patient body of research the cardiovascular community has been quietly building for decades, one of the more potent antioxidants in the food supply.

I read about the long, slowly built relationship the structure-function research has had with this vegetable.

In the context of supporting healthy circulation.

Supporting cardiovascular wellness.

Supporting the body's own cellular energy.

In people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

None of what I read was a fireworks story.

It was the opposite of one.

It was a vegetable that has been quietly studied for a long time by careful people who were not making outlandish claims.

The language being used in the careful, structure-function corner of the conversation was the language I had been waiting to find.

Supports cardiovascular wellness.

Not fixes the body. Not reverses anything. Not replaces the medication I had been on for ten years.

Just supports.

In a man whose numbers were already in the ranges his doctor wanted them in.

That was the shape of the language I had been hoping to find.

I came across a small company called Rosabella.

I want to tell you what I liked about it, plainly.

The first thing was the chemistry.

The betalain pigment is heat-sensitive.

If you process beetroot the standard way, with heat, you lose some of the compound to the heat.

Rosabella cold-presses at low temperatures because they want as much of the compound as possible to make it from the field into the capsule.

That is a slower way to make a supplement.

It costs them more.

They made the choice anyway.

After forty-one years of watching contractors take shortcuts on jobsites, I have come to pay close attention to the small decisions companies make when no one is watching.

Those decisions tell you everything.

The second thing I liked was the simplicity.

Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water.

That is the whole protocol.

I am a sixty-five-year-old retired construction supervisor.

I am not interested in a protocol that requires its own shelf.

Now I am going to tell you the most important part of this letter, the part I want every man my age who reads this to hear.

Before I added anything to what I was taking, I called my pharmacist.

His name is Carlos.

He has been the pharmacist at the small independent pharmacy in our part of town for eight years.

I have been filling my blood pressure medication there for as long as he has been there.

I called him for a specific reason.

I am on a blood pressure medication.

Beetroot supplements and blood pressure medications can interact.

The interaction is not necessarily dangerous.

It is not necessarily anything.

But it depends on the specific medication, the dose, what else you are taking, and what your prescribing physician would want.

That is exactly the kind of small, specific, individualized question that a pharmacist can answer in two minutes.

It is not a question I, as a careful patient, was going to answer on my own.

I called Carlos.

I told him what I was thinking about adding.

I told him the brand, the dosage, the protocol.

He pulled up my profile.

He looked at my prescription, the dose, the history, and the other small things I was taking.

He told me he had no concerns about adding it at the dosage on the label.

He told me to take it in the morning, with the blood pressure medication, the way the bottle suggested.

He told me to come back to him if Dr. Klein adjusted my prescription or added anything new.

He told me, before we got off the phone, that he wished more of his patients made the call I had just made.

I also called Dr. Klein's office that afternoon.

I left a message with his nurse, with the same information.

She called me back the next day to tell me Dr. Klein had no concerns and to let him know how I was doing at my next appointment.

I want to say something now, plainly, to every man my age who is reading this.

If you are on a blood pressure medication.

If you are on a cholesterol medication.

If you are on any prescription medication at all.

Please make the call.

I called Carlos.

I called Dr. Klein's office.

I would not have added anything to a prescription without those calls.

Neither should you.

The two minutes the call takes is the most important two minutes of your engagement with any supplement.

I started on a Monday in late March.

I set the bottle on the counter next to the coffee maker.

I took the two capsules with my first sip of coffee.

The first two weeks I felt nothing in particular.

I had read enough to know that bodies are different.

Some men notice something within the first week or two.

Some men take much longer.

Some men don't notice much at all.

I was prepared to land in any of those camps.

I was prepared to be honest with myself about the result.

What I noticed, I noticed slowly.

The first thing I noticed, around week six, was the workshop.

It was a Saturday morning in early May.

I went out to the garage at nine.

I had not been out there in eight months.

The bench I had been planning to build for our entryway was still on the workbench in pieces from where I had cut the rough boards in October.

I worked on it for three hours.

I cut the dadoes. I sanded the seat. I started on the joinery for the legs.

I came in at twelve with sawdust on my sleeves and asked my wife what she wanted for lunch.

She did not say anything about the sawdust.

She did not have to.

I went back out after lunch and worked for another two hours.

I finished the bench the following Saturday.

It is in our entryway now.

The second thing I noticed, around week nine, was the truck.

I had been needing to make multiple trips to the hardware store for things that I used to do in one trip.

A run for project materials had become two runs.

A weekend brush hauling had become a Saturday and a Sunday.

I had been telling myself it was my back.

It was not my back.

It was a man at sixty-five who had been quietly running out of the energy to load a truck the way he had loaded a truck for forty years.

A Saturday morning in early June, I went to the hardware store for materials for a small shed I had agreed to build for our neighbor.

I bought everything in one trip.

I loaded the truck myself.

I drove home.

I unloaded the truck myself.

I did not need to sit down on the workbench after.

I started on the shed.

The third thing I noticed was at my grandson's Little League game.

His team plays at the field at the elementary school three miles from our house.

I had been going to his games for two years.

I had been sitting in my folding chair the whole time, holding a thermos of coffee.

He had not asked me to play catch with him at the field in two years.

He had stopped asking.

A Saturday in July, around month four, my grandson walked over to me at the start of the warm-up.

He said: Grandpa. Would you throw with me?

He had a glove for me.

He had brought it without telling anybody.

I went out on the grass with him.

I threw with him for fifteen minutes.

He was nine.

He was throwing harder than he had been throwing two years before.

I caught everything he threw.

I threw it back to him at the speed I had been throwing for forty years.

I came back to my folding chair after.

I sat down.

My daughter-in-law was sitting two seats over.

She did not say anything.

She did not have to.

I had not played catch with that boy in two years.

I had been the kind of grandfather who sits in the chair.

I was, that Saturday, the kind of grandfather who goes onto the grass.

I want to be careful with what I am telling you.

I am not telling you Rosabella did any of those things on its own.

I had been walking more deliberately for several weeks before the workshop.

I had been doing the careful things I had been doing for ten years, just a little more carefully.

Something had unlocked in me the afternoon I drove home from Dr. Klein's office in February that had nothing to do with a capsule on a saucer.

There were several things changing at once, the way there are when a man in his sixties finally decides to start asking the body again, after a year of letting it sit in a recliner.

All I am telling you is that I added one small thing to a long list of things I was newly doing.

I kept everything else exactly the same — including the blood pressure medication Dr. Klein has me on.

And a stretch of months followed that was qualitatively different from the year that had preceded it.

That is the honest version.

I would not give you a cleaner one.

I went back into Dr. Klein's office for a follow-up in October.

Bloodwork the week before.

He studied the results.

He looked at me.

He said: Chuck. Your numbers are in better shape than they have been in three years. Whatever you are doing, keep doing it.

I told him about the workshop.

About the shed.

About the catch with my grandson.

About the beetroot.

I told him I had called Carlos before I started.

He nodded.

He said: that is a sensible thing to add at your dosage. There is good research on antioxidant support. Keep doing what you are doing.

I drove home from his office that afternoon.

I had been driving home from Dr. Klein's office for twelve years.

That afternoon I drove home thinking that I had been planning to retire at sixty-five and I had retired at sixty-three, and I was now sixty-five and I was, on a different chart that I was not going to be able to show anybody, in better shape than I had been at sixty-three.

I am still a retired man.

The job is gone.

But the body that I retired because of is doing more than it was doing a year and a half ago.

That is more than I had expected.

I am telling you all of this because I think you might be where I was the afternoon I sat in the truck in the parking lot two Octobers ago.

In fine shape, on paper.

In a body that has been quietly slowing down on you for years.

In a job, or a marriage, or a life, where you are quietly losing the fifteen minutes off the front of the day that you used to have.

If you are a man in your sixties — or a woman reading this for a man you love — who has been telling himself it is fine because the chart says it is fine, I want to say a few things plainly.

I do not know you.

I do not know your medications, your bloodwork, or what your doctor has told you.

Bodies are different.

Some men notice something within the first week or two of adding something like this.

Some men take much longer.

Some men don't notice much at all.

That is the honest version.

Anyone offering you a cleaner one is selling you harder than I am.

Here is how I think about Rosabella now, in the words I would actually use.

It is something the body uses.

The research community talks about it in the context of supporting healthy circulation, supporting cardiovascular wellness, and supporting the body's own cellular energy.

In people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

It is not a fix for anything.

It is not a substitute for a medication.

It is not a treatment for the quiet decline a man can have in his sixties.

It is one small thing you add to the long list of things you are already doing for yourself, alongside everything else.

Please call your pharmacist or your doctor's office before you add it.

If you are on any blood pressure medication, cholesterol medication, or anything cardiovascular at all, this is non-negotiable.

I called Carlos.

I called Dr. Klein's office.

I would not have added anything to my prescription without those calls.

Neither should you.

The people who know your chart will tell you in two minutes whether the supplement is safe for you to add given what you are already taking.

I am a sixty-five-year-old retired construction supervisor in eastern Washington.

I am not the person to ask.

Your pharmacist or your doctor is.

The company has a ninety-day satisfaction guarantee.

If you are not satisfied — for any reason at all, including just changing your mind — they refund you.

It is not tied to anything you have to prove.

If you are not satisfied, you are not satisfied.

That is the whole policy.

The beets are grown on a small operation that harvests on its own schedule.

There are stretches when they are in stock and stretches when they are not.

I mention that not to push you — please do not let anybody push you into a health decision — but so that if you go to the site and they are out, you know why.

That is all I have.

Thank you for reading this far.

I hope something here was useful to you.

I hope, somewhere today, you are throwing a ball with a nine-year-old who has been waiting to ask you for two years.

— Chuck

P.S. I want to come back to the cold-press for a second.

The betalain pigment — the deep crimson, the part the research community keeps circling back to — is heat-sensitive.

That is the chemistry of the molecule.

Process beetroot the standard way, with heat, and you lose some of the compound to the heat.

Rosabella chose the slow way.

Cold-press. Low temperatures. More of the compound through to the capsule.

It costs them money. It costs them time.

They made the choice anyway.

After forty-one years on construction sites watching subcontractors choose the slow careful way or the fast cheap way, I have come to believe that the small decisions companies make when no one is watching tell you everything.

P.P.S. Two capsules. In the morning. With a glass of water.

That is the entire protocol.

No powder to mix.

No afternoon dose to remember.

No schedule taped to the inside of the cupboard.

I keep the bottle on the counter next to the coffee maker.

I take the capsules with my first sip, alongside my blood pressure medication.

That has been the rhythm of my morning for almost a year.

A simple morning.

I have earned one.

P.P.P.S. I am going to say it one more time, because I have to.

Please.

Call your pharmacist or your doctor's office before you add anything new to what you are already taking.

Especially if you are on a blood pressure medication, a cholesterol medication, or anything cardiovascular.

They have your whole picture in front of them.

I have written you a letter.

There is a difference, and the difference matters.

The two minutes it takes to ask are the most important two minutes of your engagement with any supplement.

P.P.P.P.S. The guarantee, again, in case you skimmed.

Ninety days. Any reason at all.

You do not have to prove a single thing — not to them, not about your numbers, not about how you feel.

If you tried it and you are not satisfied, you are not satisfied.

They send the money back.

That is the whole policy.

A small-company policy.

The kind of thing companies used to do when companies still answered the phone.

P.P.P.P.P.S. The moment that took me to Dr. Klein's office in February was not anything dramatic.

It was a Tuesday afternoon in my recliner with a baseball game on the television.

The game was the World Series the previous October that I had been re-watching on a replay channel.

I had watched the same inning twice without realizing I had watched it twice.

I was sixty-four years old.

I had been sitting in a recliner in the middle of a Tuesday afternoon watching the same inning of a baseball game I had already watched two times.

The man in that recliner was not the man I had been at fifty-five.

If you are a man in your early sixties, in a recliner, in the middle of a Tuesday afternoon, watching something you have already watched, I want you to know you are not alone.

You are also not stuck there.

I am not telling you a capsule got me out of that recliner.

I am telling you it was one of several small things I added, alongside the careful work I had been doing for years, after a doctor I trusted asked me what one small additive thing I might bring back into my mornings that was not the job.

That is the question I want you to sit with.

Not the capsule.

The question.

I hope you find your own answer.

I hope you read carefully.

I hope you call your pharmacist before you add anything.

I hope, in a year from now, you are out on a grass field somewhere, throwing a ball with somebody small who is waiting to grow up.

I hope that for you.

That is the whole reason I wrote this.

— C.
