Individual experiences vary. These statements have not been evaluated by the FDA. This product is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
By Sixty-Four It Was Taking Me a Full Minute to Get From the Edge of the Bed to the Bathroom Door. 

I Had Stopped Mentioning It to Anyone, Including Myself. 

I want to start this letter on a Tuesday morning in late April.

I was in the bed at the back of our garden, the one I had planted with cosmos and zinnias and an old reliable patch of bee balm.

I had been on my knees for about fifteen minutes pulling early weeds out from around the bee balm.

The morning was still cool.

The light was just starting to clear the maple.

The dog was on the back porch watching me.

I went to stand up.

I could not stand up.

That is not exactly right.

I could stand up.

I just could not stand up the way a person stands up.

I put my right hand on the cedar bench next to me.

I shifted my weight forward over my knees.

I pushed up with my hand on the bench until my legs were under me.

I steadied myself with my hand on the bench for another moment.

Then I let go.

That whole sequence took maybe thirty seconds.

It used to take me half a second.

I was sixty-four years old, in a garden I had been kneeling in for nineteen years, and I had just used a cedar bench to stand up from a flower bed.

I went inside and made coffee.

I did not tell my husband Dale about it.

I had not been telling him about it for almost two years.

I want to tell you a few things about myself, briefly, so that you know who is writing this letter.

I am sixty-four.

I live in Bloomington, Indiana, in a small ranch house we have lived in for thirty-one years.

I worked for thirty-two years as a horticulturalist at the Bloomington Botanical Garden.

I retired three years ago.

I have been gardening every day of my life that the weather would let me since I was eleven.

I have been married to Dale for forty-one years.

We have two grown children and three grandchildren.

I am not on any prescription medication.

My annual bloodwork is in the ranges my doctor — Dr. Park, a careful, quiet woman I have been seeing for the last fourteen years — wants it in.

I walk our neighborhood with Dale most mornings.

I swim two mornings a week at the community pool.

I am, by every measure that anyone has ever measured me by, a sixty-four-year-old woman in fine shape.

That is the part I want you to hold while you read this.

The other part I want you to hold is the bottle in our medicine cabinet.

It was an over-the-counter pain reliever.

I am not going to name the brand.

I had been buying it at the grocery store, on the same trip as the eggs and the milk, on a quiet rotation that had become part of my life.

I had been reaching for it two to three mornings a week.

For a year, it had been one morning a week.

For the year before that, it had been one morning every two weeks.

Two years before that, the bottle had been in the cabinet because Dale's father had stayed with us during a recovery from his hip and had needed it.

We had kept the bottle when he left.

I had moved, slowly, over four years, from the bottle being a thing Dale's father had needed once, to the bottle being a thing I reached for two or three mornings a week.

I had not told Dale.

I had not told my daughter.

I had not told Dr. Park, because Dr. Park had not asked, and the bloodwork had been fine, and I had not wanted to bring it up.

I had also not been telling myself, in the way you do not tell yourself something you do not want to look at.

I will try to describe what the mornings had been like.

I would wake up.

I would sit on the edge of the bed for a long minute before I would put my feet on the floor.

I would put my feet on the floor and I would not stand up right away.

I would stand up and I would not walk to the bathroom right away.

I would walk to the bathroom in a careful, narrow way I had not walked in my forties.

By the time the coffee was made — which, in our house, was at about six twenty in the morning — my body had usually opened up enough to do most of what I wanted it to do.

But the mornings I had to be out in the garden early, or the mornings I had to be at the pool by seven, or the mornings the dog needed to go out before my joints had unfolded the way they needed to unfold — those mornings the bottle came down from the cabinet.

I did not call any of that a problem.

I called it being sixty-four.

I called it forty years of working on my knees in flower beds.

I called it the way a body does the small daily math of getting older.

It was all those things.

It was also something else.

I want to be honest, here, about the day I decided to do something about it.

It was a Tuesday morning at the pool, about three weeks after the morning in the garden with the bench.

I had been doing my laps.

I had gotten out of the pool.

I was standing at the foot of the ladder, holding the rail.

A woman I had seen at the pool a few times — maybe my age, maybe a year or two older — got out of the pool at the ladder next to mine.

She did not hold the rail.

She got out of the pool the way a person gets out of a pool.

She walked to her chair.

She picked up her towel.

She walked to her car.

She moved the way I had moved for the first fifty-eight years of my life.

I stood at the foot of the ladder holding the rail and watching her drive away.

I sat in the locker room for half an hour after that.

I did not cry.

I have never cried in a locker room.

I sat there and I did the slow honest math, in my head, of the woman I had been at the pool five years ago and the woman I was at the pool that morning.

I drove home.

I made coffee.

I sat at the kitchen table.

And I said, out loud, to the dog, who has heard me say a lot of important things over the years: "I'd like to feel like myself again."

That was the morning I started reading.

I read for about three weeks.

I am a careful reader.

Thirty-two years of horticulture trains you to be suspicious of any claim about what supports a plant's growth that does not have careful research underneath it.

The same suspicion applies, I have found, to claims about what supports a human body.

What I was reading for, specifically, was something that might support the way a body handles the small daily inflammation that the medical research community has come to talk about, in careful structure-function language, in the context of healthy aging.

I am going to be careful with my language here, because I am not a doctor.

The research conversation I found was not a conversation about treating any condition.

It was a conversation about supporting what the body is already trying to do.

The active compound I kept reading about was a family of pigments called betalains.

Betalains are one of the more potent antioxidants in the food supply.

The careful research community I found was studying them in the context of supporting the body's natural antioxidant balance.

Supporting the body's natural inflammatory response in healthy aging.

Supporting healthy circulation.

And supporting cellular wellness.

In people whose numbers are in the range their doctors want them in.

The careful language.

Not "fixes."

Not "treats."

Not "reverses."

Not anything about pain or disease or joints by name.

Just supports.

The most concentrated dietary source of betalains is beetroot.

The deep red kind.

The kind my grandmother used to pickle.

I read about the chemistry of the betalain pigment, which is heat-sensitive.

The pigment breaks down under the high temperatures that conventional processing uses, which is the cheaper and faster way to make a beetroot powder.

A small company called Rosabella was cold-pressing their beetroot at low temperatures.

It cost them more.

It took longer.

They were doing it anyway because more of the active compound survives the processing.

After thirty-two years at a small botanical garden, watching small careful institutions make small careful decisions when no one was watching them, I have come to believe that the way an organization handles the slow expensive parts of its work tells you everything about whether the work is real.

The other thing I liked was the simplicity.

Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water.

That is the entire protocol.

I am sixty-four.

I am not interested in another routine.

Before I added anything to my mornings, I made a phone call.

I have been buying a generic over-the-counter pain reliever from the small pharmacy two blocks from our house for almost a decade.

I called the pharmacy on a Wednesday morning and asked the pharmacist on duty whether a beetroot supplement at the dosage on the Rosabella label would be sensible to add to my routine.

Given that I was occasionally taking the OTC pain reliever I had been taking.

And that I was not on any other medication.

She asked me a few questions about my OTC use and how often.

She told me there were no concerns at the supplement dosage on the label, given what I had described.

She told me to take the capsules in the morning with food and water.

I also called Dr. Park's office that afternoon and left a message with the nurse.

She called me back the next morning to tell me Dr. Park had no concerns and to mention it at my next annual.

Both calls together took me less than ten minutes.

I want to say something now, plainly, to every reader of this letter.

If you are on any prescription medication, please make the call to your pharmacist before you add anything new to your routine.

If you are not on any prescription medication but you have been taking an over-the-counter pain reliever regularly, please call your pharmacist anyway.

Over-the-counter pain relievers have their own profiles.

Your pharmacist will tell you in two minutes whether adding a supplement to what you have been taking is sensible.

I am a retired horticulturalist.

I am not the person to ask.

Your pharmacist is.

I started on a Monday in mid-May.

I put the bottle on the counter next to the coffee maker.

I took the two capsules with my first sip of coffee.

The first two weeks I felt nothing in particular.

I had read enough, by then, to know I should not expect to.

A supplement is not a drug.

It does not switch on the way a drug switches on.

It is something the body uses, slowly, in the work the body is already doing.

I kept paying attention.

I kept reaching, on the harder mornings, for the bottle in the cabinet.

What I noticed, I noticed in two specific places.

The first was the garden.

It was a Tuesday morning in late June, about six weeks after I had started.

I was in the bee balm bed again.

I had been on my knees for about twenty minutes.

I went to stand up.

I put my hand on the cedar bench, the way I had been putting my hand on the cedar bench for two years.

And then I noticed, halfway up, that my hand was not actually carrying any weight.

My hand was on the bench because my hand had been on the bench every morning for two years.

My legs were doing the work.

I stood up.

I looked at the bench.

I tried it again, kneeling back down, getting back up without touching the bench at all.

It was not effortless.

I was not pretending I was forty.

But I stood up from a flower bed, in a garden, at sixty-four, the way a person stands up from a flower bed.

I sat on the bench for a long minute after that.

The dog watched me from the porch.

I did not say anything to Dale about it that morning.

I needed to see if it would happen again the next day.

It happened again the next day.

It happened again the day after that.

By the middle of July, I had stopped putting my hand on the bench.

I had stopped thinking about putting my hand on the bench.

The bench was just a bench in my garden again, where it had been for nineteen years, where I had been quietly using it as a piece of physical therapy equipment for two years without admitting it.

The second thing I noticed was the bottle in the cabinet.

I had been reaching for it two to three mornings a week.

Over the months after I started the supplement, the mornings I reached for it got quieter and further apart.

Not all at once.

Not on a schedule.

Not in a way I could have pointed to on a calendar.

Just over time.

I still reach for the bottle some mornings.

There are mornings my body wants what is in that bottle and I do not see any reason not to give it to my body.

The mornings I do reach for it have just gotten further apart than they were two springs ago.

I am not telling you the supplement is the reason for that.

I have been doing several things differently for a year.

I have been moving more deliberately.

I have been honest with my body in a way I had not been honest with my body for two years.

I have been telling my husband when a morning is hard, which means a morning being hard does not have to live alone inside my head anymore, which is its own kind of medicine.

The bottle is still in the cabinet.

I expect it always will be.

I just do not stand in front of the cabinet most mornings the way I used to.

I want to be careful about what I am saying here.

I am not telling you that Rosabella unfolded my mornings.

I had been doing other things since the pool.

I had been honest with myself about my body in a way I had not been honest with myself for two years.

I had been moving more deliberately.

I had been doing some slow stretching after my morning coffee.

I had been telling Dale, finally, on quiet evenings, what the last two years had actually felt like.

Something had unlocked in me at the pool that had nothing to do with a capsule on the counter.

There were several things changing at once, the way there are when a careful woman finally watches another woman her age walk to her car without holding a rail.

What I can tell you is that I added one small thing to a list of small things I was newly doing.

I kept everything else exactly the same.

And the months that followed were qualitatively different from the two years that preceded them.

I went into Dr. Park's office in October for my annual.

She did the bloodwork the week before.

She reviewed the results with me.

She did the rest of the physical.

She said: "Your numbers are in the same good place they've been for years. You are moving more easily through the exam than you did last year. I do not know what you have been doing. Whatever it is, please continue."

I told her about the woman at the pool.

I told her about the garden bench.

I told her about the bottle in the cabinet and how the mornings I reached for it had gotten further apart over the last several months.

I told her about the beetroot.

She nodded.

She said: "The research on antioxidant support for the body's inflammatory balance is well-established. That was a sensible thing to add. Keep doing it. Come back in a year."

She did not write me a prescription.

She did not put anything new in my chart.

She wrote down the brand name on a piece of paper for her own reference and put it in her white coat pocket.

I am telling you all of this because I think you might be a woman where I was two springs ago.

In a quiet two years.

Reaching for a bottle in a medicine cabinet a little more often than you want to be.

Putting your hand on a bench, on a counter, on a wall, on the back of a chair, when you stand up.

Watching the small daily things your body used to do without thinking about them quietly become things your body has to think about.

Your bloodwork fine.

Your doctor kind.

Your husband — if you have one — watching you, the way husbands watch, without saying anything about what he is seeing.

If that has been your two years, I want to say a few things plainly.

I am not telling you that beetroot will do for you what something has done for me.

I don't know you.

I don't know your body.

I don't know what you have been taking, prescribed or otherwise.

Bodies are different.

Some women notice something within the first month of adding something like this.

Some women take much longer.

Some women don't notice much at all.

What I am telling you is that there is a careful, slow, structure-function corner of the research conversation that talks about beetroot in the language I had been waiting for.

Not fixes.

Not treats.

Just supports.

In the body's natural antioxidant balance.

In the body's natural inflammatory response in healthy aging.

In healthy circulation.

In cellular wellness.

In people whose numbers are already in the range their doctors want them in.

If that shape of language interests you, please make the call before you add anything.

Call your pharmacist.

Call your doctor's office.

If you have been taking any over-the-counter pain reliever regularly — even occasionally — please tell your pharmacist what you have been taking when you call.

Those medications have their own profiles, and the pharmacist who fills your prescriptions is the right person to confirm whether a supplement is sensible to add to what you have already been doing.

I am a retired horticulturalist.

I am not the person to ask.

Your pharmacist is.

The brand I added to my morning is Rosabella.

1,300 milligrams of organic beetroot per serving.

Cold-pressed at low temperatures.

Grown by a small farming community in Lancaster County.

Two capsules with a glass of water in the morning.

That is the entire protocol.

It is not a fix for anything.

It is not a substitute for any prescription medication, any over-the-counter remedy, or any other treatment a doctor might recommend.

It is not a treatment for the small daily aches a body can have between sixty and sixty-five.

It is one small thing some women add to the long list of things they are already doing for themselves, alongside everything else.

P.S. Rosabella has a ninety-day satisfaction guarantee.

Any reason at all.

If you tried it and you are not satisfied, you are not satisfied.

They refund you.

That is the whole policy.

I appreciated that kind of policy at sixty-four.

P.P.S. They're a small operation working with a farming community in Lancaster County.

The cold-pressing process takes longer than the conventional methods, so they sometimes run out between harvests.

I keep an extra bottle in our cupboard now.

P.P.P.S. I went back to the pool last Tuesday morning.

The woman I had watched a year and a half ago was not there.

I have not seen her at the pool in several months.

I do not know where she went or what her life has been doing in the meantime.

I did not hold the ladder rail when I got out.

I walked to my chair, picked up my towel, and walked to my car.

I sat in the car for a minute before I started the engine.

I thought about telling her, if she ever comes back, what watching her walk to her car had done for me on a Tuesday morning in April when I was sixty-four years old and a bottle in my medicine cabinet was getting opened more often than I wanted to admit.

I think, if she comes back, I will tell her.

She is the closest thing to a stranger-as-mirror that I have had in my life.

She does not know what she did for me.

She would probably tell me, if I told her, that she does not deserve any credit.

That she was just getting out of the pool.

That she did not even see me.

I would tell her she is wrong, and that she will have to take my word for it, because at sixty-four I have learned that some of the most important strangers in your life are the ones who never knew they were there.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
He Came In for a Box of Screws. What He Said Before He Left Changed How I See My Husband.

I went to the hardware store on a Tuesday afternoon in October because we needed weatherstripping for the back door and I kept putting it off and it had gotten cold enough that I couldn't anymore.

It's not a chain. It's the kind of store where there's a bell above the door and the floors are wood and they still have things in bins that you scoop yourself. 

I've been going there since we moved to this town twenty-two years ago. It smells like oil and sawdust and the particular kind of dust that settles on things that have been in one place for a long time.

I was in the back, looking for the weatherstripping, when I heard someone ask for help finding a box of screws.

Not an unusual request. But the voice caught my attention.

Not because of what it said. Because of how it moved.

There are voices that stay in one place when they talk. 

And there are voices that come from people who are comfortable in their body — people who move when they speak, who lean and gesture and turn without thinking about it. 

This man had that kind of voice.

 Easy. Unhurried. Like the words came out of someone who was used to doing things with his hands.

I came around the end of the aisle and saw him.

He was older than I'd expected from the voice. Late sixties, maybe seventy. 

White hair, good posture. He was reaching up to a top shelf — not straining, not hesitating — just reaching. Like height had never been something he'd had to negotiate with.

The young man helping him was probably twenty-five. He was the one reaching carefully, watching his footing on the step stool.

 The older man stood below and waited and didn't need to hold anything for balance.

I found the weatherstripping. I took longer than I needed to finding it.

The older man got his screws and looked at the box and said something to the young man about the gauge, and the young man said "I didn't know that," and the older man said "now you do," and there was nothing condescending about it.

It was just someone passing something on.

He moved toward the register and I moved toward the register and we ended up in line at the same time and I did what I do when I'm standing next to someone in a quiet store, which is make a small comment about nothing.

I said something about the cold coming early this year.

He said yes, but he didn't mind it. Said his joints used to, but not anymore.

I don't know what made me say the next thing. Maybe it was the way he'd stood at that shelf without holding on. 

Maybe it was the way he'd waited without shifting his weight. Maybe I'd been carrying something around for a while and it was looking for a door.

I said: "My husband used to say the same thing. That the cold didn't bother him."

He looked at me. Not intrusively. Just — attentively.

"Used to?" he said.

"He's been having a harder time the last few years," I said. "The doctor says his cholesterol numbers have been drifting. He's been eating better, walking more. It just — doesn't seem to keep up with what his body wants to do."

The man nodded slowly. Not with pity. With recognition.

"My wife said the same thing about me," he said. "About four years ago."

He paid for his screws. I paid for my weatherstripping. He held the door for me on the way out — not in a hurry, just a reflex — and we stood on the sidewalk for a few minutes in the way that strangers sometimes do when neither of them is in a particular rush.

He told me that four years ago his doctor had mentioned that his cholesterol numbers were trending in a direction they'd both rather not see. 

He'd done the same things my husband was doing. Diet. Exercise. Fish oil. His wife had been the one to push him to look harder. 

She'd read about beetroot — the research on beetroot for cardiovascular wellness had apparently been building for decades, peer-reviewed journals, consistent enough that she'd taken it seriously.

He'd been skeptical.

His wife had brought home a bottle of something called Rosabella. Small company. Cold-pressed at low temperatures, she'd explained, because the active compounds in beetroot — the betalains and the dietary nitrates — are heat-sensitive, and most processing destroys a good portion of them before the capsule is ever sealed. She'd done her homework.

He told his doctor what he was thinking of adding. 

His doctor said it was a sensible thing to try alongside everything else he was already doing. Not instead of anything. Just alongside.

He started taking two capsules in the morning with a glass of water.

"And?" I said.

He shrugged. Not dismissively. More like the shrug of someone who has stopped needing to convince anyone of anything.

"I reached the top shelf," he said. "I didn't think about it. That's the whole story."

I drove home thinking about my husband.

Not about bloodwork. Not about numbers. About the way he used to get up from the couch — just stood up, didn't need a moment, didn't need his hand on the armrest. 

About the way he used to walk ahead of me in parking lots and wait, not because I was slow but because his natural pace was just a little faster than mine.

About when that changed. How gradually it changed. How easy it is to call a gradual thing aging and leave it there.

His doctor had mentioned at his last visit that his cholesterol levels were something to keep watching. Not alarming. 

Just — worth attention. He'd left the appointment with instructions to keep doing what he was doing and come back in six months.

He came home and told me all this in a way that meant he was fine, it was fine, nothing needed to happen.

I called the doctor's office the next week. 

I asked whether it would be reasonable for him to add a beetroot supplement to what he was already doing. I described what I'd read about the betalains, the cold-pressing, the research on cardiovascular wellness.

The nurse called back the next day. The doctor said it was a reasonable thing to try. Not a replacement for anything. Just something to add.

I ordered a bottle of Rosabella. One capsule — 1,300 milligrams of organic beetroot, cold-pressed — every morning with his orange juice. Added to everything he was already doing, not replacing any of it.

That was about four months ago.

Last month we walked to dinner. It's maybe six blocks from the house. A hill on the way back that I've always found easier than he has.

He didn't mention the hill.

I noticed. I didn't say anything. But I noticed.

His doctor saw him for the follow-up appointment three weeks ago. She looked at his chart and looked at him and said his bloodwork was in better shape than it had been in a while. She didn't say anything dramatic. Doctors don't, usually. She just said: keep doing what you're doing.

That's what he's doing.

I'm not a doctor. I'm not in healthcare. I don't know exactly what's happening at whatever level it's happening at. 

I'm one person who went to the hardware store for weatherstripping and came home thinking about my husband in a different way than I had in years.

If you've been watching something similar at home — the slower mornings, the way they sit down now instead of just sitting, the hill that used to be nothing — I'd say it's worth a conversation with your doctor. Ask about adding something, not replacing anything.

The man at the hardware store asked his doctor. I asked mine. That's the first step and it's a quick one.

Talk to your doctor or pharmacist before adding any supplement, especially if you or your husband is already on medication for blood pressure or heart health.

 That call takes ten minutes and it's the right place to start.

The brand I used is Rosabella. 1,300 milligrams of organic beetroot per serving, cold-pressed at low temperatures to preserve the betalains. 

Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water. Added to whatever else you're already doing — not replacing any of it.

P.S. Rosabella has a 90-day money-back guarantee. If you're not satisfied for any reason, every penny back. I'd give it at least a few weeks before forming an opinion — some people notice something within the first week or two, others take longer. Bodies are different.

P.P.S. They're a small company. They harvest and bottle on their own schedule, so there are times when supply runs tight. I keep a spare bottle in the house for that reason. Added to whatever else he's already doing — not replacing any of it — but I'd rather not have a gap.

P.P.P.S. My husband doesn't know I called the doctor's office before ordering the first bottle. He knows I ordered it. He knows he takes it every morning. He doesn't know I stood in the hardware store parking lot on a Tuesday in October with weatherstripping under my arm, thinking: that could be him again. That's what I was actually buying.

P.P.P.P.S. Decades of peer-reviewed research on beetroot for cardiovascular wellness. The betalains, the dietary nitrates, the antioxidant support. None of it is new. Some of it is just new to us.

P.P.P.P.P.S. The man at the hardware store reached the top shelf without thinking about it. That's the whole story. I think about that a lot.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
My Sister Called on a Thursday Afternoon. She Wasn't Calling About Anything. 

That's How I Knew Something Was on Her Mind.

My sister Diane calls on Sunday mornings, usually. That's our time. 

We've been doing it since our mother died eleven years ago — just fell into it, the way you fall into things when you're both trying to hold something together that used to be held by someone else.

When she called on a Thursday afternoon in February, I picked up on the second ring.

"Everything okay?" I said.

"Everything's fine," she said. "I just felt like talking."

Diane is sixty-one. She lives in Raleigh. 

She teaches fourth grade, has for thirty years, and she has the particular quality that good elementary school teachers develop after long enough, which is that she never quite says the real thing first. 

She leads up to it. 

She lets you get settled before she hands you the thing she actually called to hand you.

We talked about her students for a while. 

We talked about her husband Frank's retirement, which is coming up in the spring and which she is — she admitted — slightly terrified about. 

We talked about my daughter's new apartment and whether the neighborhood was as safe as my daughter claimed.

And then there was a small pause.

Not a dramatic pause. Just the kind that opens up when someone has been working up to something and has finally decided to say it.

"I went to the doctor on Tuesday," she said. "Just the regular checkup."

"And?"

"And everything's fine," she said. "Mostly."

I know my sister. I waited.

"She mentioned my cholesterol," she said. "Said the numbers have been creeping. Nothing alarming. Just — something to keep an eye on."

I knew exactly what that felt like. The way the doctor says it — careful, not urgent, more of a heads-up than a warning. 

The way you drive home from that appointment turning it over. 

Not scared, exactly. Just aware, in a new way, that something is moving in a direction you'd rather it didn't.

"I've heard that before," I said.

"I know you have," she said. "That's part of why I called."

Two years ago, my doctor said almost exactly the same thing to me.

My numbers had been drifting for about three years by then. Nothing dramatic. Just the slow kind of drift that shows up in the same column of your bloodwork every year, a little higher, a little more of a frown from the doctor, a little more emphasis on the diet conversation you'd already been having.

I was doing what you do. I'd cut back on the things you cut back on. I was walking every morning. 

I was taking fish oil. I'd added oatmeal to my routine in a way that I would describe as dutiful rather than enthusiastic.

The numbers would come down a little. Then drift back. Then come down. Then drift.

My doctor, at that appointment two years ago, said: let's keep watching. 

She mentioned that if it kept going, we might want to talk about other options. She said it gently. I heard it clearly.

I didn't want other options. I wanted to find something I hadn't tried yet.

I'm not sure exactly how I found Rosabella. 

I think I was reading something about beetroot — I'd seen a few things here and there over the years, peer-reviewed research on beetroot for cardiovascular wellness, consistent enough across enough time that I'd started taking it seriously. 

Rosabella was a small company, and what caught my attention was how they processed it. 

The compounds in beetroot that the research kept pointing to — the betalains, the dietary nitrates — are heat-sensitive. 

Rosabella cold-presses at low temperatures specifically to preserve them, from the time the beetroot leaves the ground to the time the capsule is sealed. 

That kind of deliberateness tends to mean someone has actually thought about what they're doing.

I called my doctor's office before I ordered anything.

I want to say that plainly because I think it matters: I called first. 

I described what I was reading about, what I was thinking of adding, and asked whether it made sense given everything I was already doing. 

The nurse called me back the next day. The doctor said it sounded like a reasonable thing to try. 

Not a replacement for anything. Just something to add alongside what was already in place.

So that's what I did. Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water. 1,300 milligrams of organic beetroot, cold-pressed. 

Added to the fish oil and the oatmeal and the morning walks — not instead of any of it.

I've been taking it for almost two years now.

At my last appointment, my doctor looked at my chart and looked at me and said my bloodwork was in better shape than it had been in a while. 

She seemed pleased in that quiet, professional way doctors have when something is going in the right direction and they don't want to make promises. 

I walked out of that office feeling like the thing I'd added was earning its place.

I can't tell you with certainty that it was the beetroot. I'm not a scientist.

I'm not in healthcare. I'm a fifty-eight-year-old woman who went looking for one more thing to try and found something that, going by what my doctor said at my last visit, seems to be worth trying. 

That's as far as I'll take it. I think that's as far as anyone should take it.

I told Diane all of this on the phone. 

Not in a rush. The way you tell a story to someone who already knows most of the people in it.

She listened the way she listens — without interrupting, the way she probably listens to her students when they need to find their own way to the end of a thought.

When I finished she said: "And your doctor was fine with it?"

"She was the one who told me to keep doing what I was doing," I said.

A pause.

"I'd want to ask my own doctor," Diane said.

"That's exactly what I'd tell you to do," I said. "Call the office, describe what you're thinking of adding, let them weigh in. It took me one phone call and a day to get a callback. That's it."

She said she'd think about it.

I know my sister. That means she'd already decided.

I'm writing this down because Diane asked me to. She said she wanted to be able to read it again instead of trying to remember what I'd said. 

And if you've been in a similar place — the slow drift, the annual conversation with your doctor, doing everything you're supposed to do and watching the needle move anyway — I think it's worth knowing that there are things worth asking about. 

Not as replacements for what you're already doing. Just as additions. One careful conversation with your doctor is the right place to start and it's a shorter conversation than you'd expect.

The brand is Rosabella. 1,300 milligrams of organic beetroot per serving, cold-pressed at low temperatures. Two capsules in the morning with a glass of water. 

Added to whatever else you're already doing — not replacing any of it.

P.S. Rosabella has a 90-day money-back guarantee. If you're not satisfied for any reason, every penny back. 

I'd give it a few weeks at minimum before forming an opinion. Some people notice something within the first week or two — others take longer. 

Bodies are different, and I think patience is part of the deal with anything like this.

P.P.S. They're a small operation. They harvest and bottle on their own schedule, so availability isn't always guaranteed. I keep a spare bottle in the house now just because I'd rather not have a gap.

P.P.P.S. Diane called me on a Sunday morning about three weeks after that Thursday call. Our regular time. She said she'd talked to her doctor, who said it was a sensible thing to try alongside everything else she was already doing. She'd ordered a bottle. She'd been taking it for about two weeks.

I asked how she was feeling.

She said: "I don't know yet. But I feel like I did something."

I understood that completely. Sometimes that's the whole thing — just the feeling of having done something deliberate, something considered, instead of waiting for the drift to continue.

P.P.P.P.S. Decades of peer-reviewed research on beetroot for cardiovascular wellness. The betalains. The dietary nitrates. The antioxidant support. It's not new science. It just took me a while to find my way to it.

P.P.P.P.P.S. She teaches fourth grade. Thirty years. She reads the research before she makes a decision about anything. She called her doctor before she ordered a single capsule. If you're the kind of person who does things the careful way — that's who I'm talking to. That's who I am too.

headline: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
I Met a Man at the DMV Who Was 78 Years Old and Renewing His Driver's License. What He Said About His Blood Pressure Pills Made Me Stop Eating My Lunch.

I was at the DMV last spring.

I had been there for over an hour.

I had a sandwich in my bag and the kind of long flat afternoon that only the DMV can produce.

There was a man sitting two seats over from me.

He had to be in his late 70s.

White hair, trimmed bearuskay d, button-down shirt tucked into khaki pants. The kind of older man who still irons his own clothes.

He had a thick folder in his lap with what looked like every document he owned organized into clear plastic sleeves.

He smiled at me when I sat down.

We did the small-talk dance for a couple of minutes.

He was renewing his driver's license. So was I. He'd been coming to this DMV since 1979.

I told him I was 62. He told me he was 78.

I said, "You don't look 78."

He laughed.

He said, "Honey, that's the nicest thing I've heard all week, but I've earned every one of these years and I'm not going to pretend I haven't."

I liked him immediately.

We sat there for another half-hour waiting for our numbers to be called.

I pulled my sandwich out and ate half of it. He pulled out a paperback and read for a while.

At some point — I don't remember exactly how the conversation drifted there — we started talking about getting older.

He told me he'd been thinking a lot lately about the difference between how a body looks on paper and how it actually feels to live in it.

I asked him what he meant.

He said his bloodwork had been excellent for 14 years.

His blood pressure had been well-controlled.

His doctor was a good doctor — careful, attentive, took his time at appointments.

He said he had no complaints about his medical care.

But somewhere along the way, he said, he had stopped feeling like himself.

Not because of anything wrong with the care he was getting.

The care was doing exactly what it was designed to do.

It was just that the care had a limit.

Bloodwork could tell him his numbers were fine.

It couldn't tell him whether he felt like a person.

That, he said, was on him.

He told me he wasn't anti-medicine.

He told me he wasn't one of those people who thinks doctors are out to get him.

He had been on his blood pressure medication for 14 years and had taken it religiously the whole time.

He said the medication was doing exactly what the doctor had prescribed it to do.

But somewhere along the way, he said, he had stopped feeling like himself.

Not all at once. Slowly. Click by click.

The energy went first.

Then the spring in his step.

Then his willingness to plan things in the evenings, because the evenings were when his battery was empty.

He said his bloodwork was always fine.

He said his blood pressure was always fine.

But the man was not fine.

He said his doctor would look at the chart and say "you're doing great" — and that was accurate. The numbers were great. The chart was doing its job.

But he would walk out of the office feeling worse than he had walked in, and the chart hadn't asked about that part, because the chart wasn't designed to.

He said one morning, after about ten years of this, he had a thought he couldn't shake.

He said, "I realized the doctor's job and my job were two different jobs. The doctor's job was to keep my numbers in a safe range. The doctor was doing that beautifully. My job — the part nobody else was going to do for me — was figuring out how I actually felt inside this body and whether there was anything I could be doing alongside the medical care to support the parts the chart wasn't measuring."

He said the thought had bothered him for a year before he did anything about it.

He told me he had brought it up with his doctor.

He had asked, plainly: is there anything I could be doing alongside this medication that might support my body's overall energy and circulation, beyond just keeping the numbers down?

He said the doctor thought about it for a minute.

The doctor said the medication was doing its job in its lane.

The doctor said that for the other stuff — the general energy, the cellular-level support, the feeling-like-yourself part — there were some sensible things he could explore, and to come back and tell the doctor what he wanted to try.

He said he thanked his doctor and meant it.

The doctor wasn't dismissing him. The doctor was being honest about what the prescription was designed to do and what it wasn't.

He said the conversation was, in his words, "one of the most useful ten minutes I've spent with a doctor in 20 years."

Because the doctor had been clear about what was the medicine's lane and what was his lane.

And he had walked out knowing what his lane looked like for the first time.

He told me he started reading.

He told me he wasn't a fast reader and he wasn't a scientific person.

But he read for a long time.

What he ended up coming back to, over and over, was beetroot.

He said the compounds that matter in beets are called betalains. The deep red pigments. They support the body's natural antioxidant balance and cellular energy.

He said he had tried beetroot before. Powder. Juice. He'd felt nothing.

He'd assumed beetroot was overrated.

What he hadn't known — and this was the thing he kept emphasizing to me — was that betalains are sensitive to heat. Most beetroot supplements are processed at high temperatures because high heat is fast and cheap. The betalains get damaged before the powder ever reaches the bottle.

He said this was why he hadn't felt anything from the powders.

He'd been taking damaged product.

The processing that preserves the betalains, he learned, is cold-pressing at low temperatures.

He told me he'd looked for a beetroot supplement that explicitly described its processing approach that way.

That filter cut the field considerably.

He'd settled on a small company called Rosabella.

I asked him what he did next.

He said he called his doctor's office before ordering anything.

He told the nurse exactly what he was thinking about — a beetroot supplement, cold-pressed, for general antioxidant and cellular energy support — and asked whether there was any reason not to add it given his medication.

The nurse came back and said the doctor had no concerns and thought it was a sensible addition.

The doctor told him to keep taking his blood pressure medication exactly as prescribed.

He told me he wanted to be very clear about something.

He said, "I never went off my medication. I didn't change a single thing my doctor told me to do. I added one thing. I called the doctor first. That phone call was the most important step of the whole thing. Not the supplement. The phone call."

He said that twice.

I wrote it down on the back of my DMV ticket.

I asked him what happened after he started taking it.

He said the first few weeks, nothing.

He said he had almost stopped, because he had been disappointed by supplements before.

He said somewhere in the second month, he started waking up without the heavy feeling that had been sitting on his chest in the morning for years.

It wasn't dramatic.

He didn't suddenly feel like he was 40.

He just stopped having to sit on the edge of the bed for two minutes before he could stand up.

That was the first thing he noticed.

He said by the third month, he was reading in the evenings again. He'd put his book down sometime in his late 60s and just stopped picking it up. He'd assumed he was done with books.

He said he hadn't been done with books.

He'd been done with the feeling of having no battery left in the evenings.

By the time he made it to his next check-up, his blood pressure was, in the doctor's words, "in better shape than it's been in years."

The doctor was pleased.

He told the doctor what he'd been doing. The doctor said to keep at it.

He looked at me across the row of plastic DMV chairs.

He said, "I'm going to tell you something, because you have a kind face and I think you might be the kind of person who needs to hear it."

I waited.

He said, "At our age, the medical system does a really important job. It keeps the numbers in range. It catches the things that need catching. It's doing that for me right now and I'm grateful for it."

"But there's a part of being a person that the medical system can't really see, because it isn't designed to see it. The general energy. The way you feel walking through your own house. The willingness to read a book at night. That part is our job. Nobody else is going to do that part for us."

"What I'd say to you is — pay attention to how you actually feel inside your own body. Tell your doctor about it. A good doctor will take it seriously and help you think through what's the medicine's lane and what's your lane. Then go do your part."

He smiled.

His number got called a minute later.

He gathered his folder. Stood up. Tipped an imaginary hat at me.

He walked to the counter.

I watched him walk.

He walked like a man whose body was still listening to him.

Sixteen years older than me.

I sat there for another twenty minutes waiting for my own number.

I thought about my husband, who is 64, and who has been telling me for about a year now that he's "just tired."

I thought about myself, and about the way I sit on the edge of the bed for a minute in the morning before I stand up, and how I had been telling myself for about two years that this was normal.

I thought about the lane I'd been leaving empty.

I thought about how I had never asked my doctor whether there might be anything I could explore alongside my prescription to support the parts of my life that weren't really about the chart.

My number got called.

I renewed my license.

I drove home with the back of the DMV ticket in my pocket.

That night, I sat with my laptop after dinner and I looked up Rosabella.

I read everything on the site.

I read it twice.

The thing I kept coming back to, just like the man at the DMV had said, was the processing.

Cold-pressed at low temperatures.

Plain language on the bottle.

No grand claims.

The next morning, I called my own doctor's office.

I told the nurse who picked up exactly what I was thinking about. A beetroot supplement. Cold-pressed. For general antioxidant and cellular energy support.

I asked whether — given my age, given my own blood pressure pill, given my last bloodwork — there was any reason I shouldn't add it.

She put me on hold for a few minutes.

She came back and said the doctor had no concerns at all.

She said the doctor often recommended general antioxidant support for patients in my age range.

She said to make sure I keep taking my blood pressure medication exactly as prescribed.

I want to stop here and say this clearly, because I think it's the most important part of the whole story.

That phone call was the most important step.

Not the DMV conversation. Not the man with the folder. Not the supplement.

The phone call.

If you take one thing from what I'm sharing, take that.

You call your doctor's office before you add anything to what you're already taking. Always. Four minutes. The most important step in this whole process. Don't skip it.

I ordered the bottle the same afternoon.

I want to tell you what I noticed, and roughly when.

The first few weeks, I didn't see anything I could point to.

I had already been warned to expect this.

Sometime in the second month, the bed-edge wait stopped.

I didn't notice it the first morning. I noticed it the third or fourth morning when I realized I was already standing up at the dresser and I hadn't sat for the usual minute.

Then later, I started reading in the evenings again — something I had stopped doing without noticing I'd stopped.

By spring, I had signed up for a watercolor class at the community center.

By summer, my husband and I were planning a trip we hadn't taken in five years.

My most recent check-up was, in my doctor's words, "in good shape, keep doing what you're doing."

I am still on my blood pressure medication.

I have not changed anything my doctor told me to take.

I am also, for the first time in a few years, reading my book at night instead of falling asleep in front of the TV by 8:30.

The man at the DMV was right about the lane.

P.S. If you've been carrying around the feeling that your numbers are fine but you yourself are not quite fine — that there's a part of feeling like a person that the chart isn't measuring — the first thing I'd say is talk to your doctor about it. Not about supplements. About you. About what you've been feeling alongside the medical care you're already getting. A good doctor will take that seriously and help you think through what the prescription is doing and what you might add to support the parts the prescription isn't designed to address. Don't let yourself wave it off as aging. Some of it might be. Some of it might not.

P.P.S. If you decide to try Rosabella, call your doctor's office before you start. Like I did. Just to confirm there's no reason not to add it given whatever else you're taking. It takes one phone call. Most nurses will get you an answer the same day. That phone call is the most important step.

Then put the bottle next to the coffee maker. Two capsules with water in the morning. Don't change anything else you're taking. Just add this one thing.

Give it 60 days before you decide anything. Some people notice something within the first few weeks. Others take longer. Bodies are different. Be patient with yours.

P.P.P.S. Rosabella has a 90-day money-back guarantee. If you're not satisfied for any reason, every penny back. No questions asked. That's a sensible policy. It gives you long enough to actually see whether it helps before you decide.

P.P.P.P.S. Rosabella is a small operation. They harvest and bottle on their own schedule, so I keep an extra bottle in the cupboard. They currently offer a multi-bottle option that I went with myself. Worth a look.

headline: I've Seen Too Much to Let My Mom Sleep Behind a Green Light.
body:
I've been a firefighter for 7 years. After the call that changed everything last May, the first thing I did was replace every detector in my own house.

The second thing I did kept me up at night until I drove across town and did something about it.

My parents.

Dad's 71. Mom's 68. They've lived in the same house since 1987. It's the house I grew up in. The house my kids call Grandma and Grandpa's house.

Furnace is original. Replaced once, maybe fifteen years ago. Water heater is old. Gas stove they've had since I was in middle school.

After that May call, after I saw a brand new detector sit silent at 67 PPM while two kids breathed poison in their sleep, I couldn't stop thinking about my parents' house.

Because I knew what was on their wall.

I bought them a CO detector years ago. Plugged it in myself. Showed my dad the green light. Told him they were safe.

That was the last time I thought about it.

The following Saturday I drove over. Didn't tell them why. Just said I wanted to check on a few things.

I walked straight to the hallway. There it was. Same spot I'd installed it. Green light glowing.

I pulled it off the wall and turned it over.

Manufactured 2016.

Eight years old. The sensor lifespan on these units is five to seven years. That sensor had been dead for at least a year. Possibly two or three.

But the green light was still on.

Because the green light doesn't monitor the sensor. It monitors the power source. As long as the unit is plugged in, that light glows. Whether the sensor is working or dead. Whether there's 0 PPM in the air or 100.

My parents had been looking at a dead detector with a green light for years and believing they were protected.

Because I told them they were.

I plugged in my work meter. I walked through the house.

0 PPM everywhere. They were fine. For now.

But that furnace is old. That water heater is old. If either one develops a crack, and they do, all the time, I see it on calls constantly, carbon monoxide would leak into the house and that dead detector wouldn't make a sound.

My mom would get headaches. She'd blame it on not sleeping well. My dad would feel dizzy. He'd blame it on his blood pressure medication.

They'd never suspect the air. Because the green light told them the air was fine.

I've seen this play out. I've responded to this exact call. Elderly person living alone or with a spouse. Symptoms for weeks or months. Chalked up to aging. Chalked up to medication side effects. Chalked up to everything except the furnace that's been slowly poisoning them.

By the time someone figures it out, if someone figures it out, the damage is done.

I looked at my mom standing in the kitchen watching me pull things off the wall.

"You worry too much," she said.

I told her about the family on Maple Street. Mom, dad, three kids. Brand new detectors. Green lights glowing. CO built all night. All five of them were gone by morning.

I told her about the 70 PPM threshold. How detectors are designed to stay silent at 30, 40, 50, 60 PPM. Levels that are dangerous for anyone, but especially for people over 65 with heart conditions or respiratory issues.

I told her the detector I'd bought her years ago, the one I told her would keep them safe, had been dead for at least a year.

She went quiet for a long time.

Then she said, "Well. Fix it."

I installed Steadfast Haven throughout their house. One on each floor. One near the furnace. One near the kitchen stove.

It's what my crew uses. What HVAC techs use in their own homes. Alarms at 10 PPM. Shows the actual reading on a digital display. Dual sensors for CO and natural gas.

It took me twenty minutes. No ladder. No tools. Just plugged them in.

The displays lit up. 0 PPM. 0 PPM. 0 PPM. 0 PPM.

My dad watched the numbers appear and nodded. Didn't say much. He's not the type. But he stood there and looked at that screen for a while.

My mom called me the next morning.

"The screens all say zero. Is that good?"

That's the best call I've ever gotten, Mom.

She checks them every morning now. She told my sister it makes her feel like I'm there even when I'm not. Like someone's watching the house for her.

My sister called me that afternoon. "Can you get the same thing for my in-laws?"

I ordered two more packs that week. One for my sister's in-laws. One for my wife's parents.

Then my buddy at the station asked me to get one for his mom. Then another guy asked for his grandmother.

That's how it started.

Now I've lost count of how many families I've told about Haven. How many parents and grandparents are sleeping behind a real number instead of a dead sensor and a green light.

Every time I visit my parents now, I glance at the display in the hallway. 0 PPM.

Every time my mom calls me about something unrelated, she mentions it. "Screens still say zero." She's proud of them. Like they're part of the house now. Part of her routine.

She doesn't know what I've seen on the job. I've never told her about the worst calls. The ones where we're too late. The ones where the detector was on the wall and the green light was still glowing and it didn't matter.

She doesn't need to know.

She just needs four zeros on four screens.

And I need to know that the house I grew up in, the house my kids play in on weekends, is actually protected. Not by a dead sensor and a light. By a number I can see and trust.

If your parents are in their sixties or seventies. If they live in a house with a furnace that's more than ten years old. If you bought them a CO detector at some point and haven't thought about it since.

Go check it. Turn it over. Look at the manufacture date.

If it's older than five years, the sensor is almost certainly dead. The green light will still be on. The test button will still beep. And it will detect nothing.

Your mom will tell you everything's fine. Your dad will say you worry too much.

They're not wrong about the worrying. But they're wrong about the detector.

Replace it with something that shows a real number. Something that doesn't go dark when the sensor dies. Something that alarms at 10 PPM instead of waiting until 70.

I've seen too much to let the people I love sleep behind a green light.

I linked the one I put in my parents' house below. 💛

headline: The room you tested no longer exists.
body:
I've been finishing basements for nineteen years. Framing, insulation, drywall, flooring. Hundreds of them. I used to think of a basement finish as purely additive — you're taking a cold, raw space and making it livable. Every upgrade is an improvement.

Then a client called me six months after I'd finished his basement. His kids played down there every afternoon. He'd bought a continuous monitor after a neighbor mentioned it. His reading was 9.1 pCi/L.

He asked if my renovation could have changed his radon levels.

I didn't have a good answer. So I looked into it. What I found is something I should have understood a long time ago, and something every basement contractor in America understands but doesn't tell clients.

An unfinished basement has natural air exchange. The rim joists leak. The slab has gaps. Radon seeps up from the soil but also has places to go.

When you finish a basement, you eliminate that exchange. Spray-foam the rim joists. Insulate. Drywall. Lay flooring. From an energy standpoint, you're doing exactly what you're supposed to do. From a radon standpoint, you've just turned the space into a sealed insulated box sitting directly over the soil.

The radon that was venting out now stays. It concentrates in the room you just made warm enough and comfortable enough to actually spend time in.

The pre-renovation test describes a room that no longer exists.

Most people in this position have one of two things to go off. A charcoal test from when they bought the house. Or nothing at all. Either way, what they're holding is a number from a different room — the unfinished version of the space, with the natural air exchange, before any of the work that changed everything about how the air moves down there.

The question that hits, usually, isn't what do I do next. It's how long has this been going on. Because radon doesn't show up one day and announce itself. It was there last winter. It was probably there the winter before that. You just didn't have any way to know.

That's not a mistake. Radon has no smell, no color, nothing your body can detect. Your instincts work fine. There's nothing for them to catch.

A continuous monitor sits in the room and reads the air every hour. Real-time number. Not a snapshot from closing. Not a guess. Just what's actually there, right now.

If the number is low, you know. Not assume — know.

If it's elevated, you find out now instead of years from now.

I'm not in the business of frightening anyone. Most homes test fine. But if you finished your basement in the last decade and you've never tested it as a finished basement, you're holding a number from a room that doesn't exist anymore. There's no reason to keep doing that.

The article linked below is the most thorough write-up I've found on this — what's actually happening in finished basements, what the trade has known and hasn't said, and what the numbers look like in real homes. If you finished yours in the last decade, read it tonight.

headline: Caribbean Resort Investigation
body:
I've been an investigative journalist for over a decade.

Six years ago, I stopped letting my family check into hotel rooms I haven't personally tested first.

Not because I'm paranoid.

Because I've spent the last six years documenting what's actually killing American tourists at Caribbean resorts. And what every coroner has missed.

It started with a story in May 2019.

A family of four. Found dead in their room at a Bahia Principe resort in Punta Cana, Dominican Republic.

Husband. Wife. Two daughters — ages 7 and 9.

The cause of death listed by Dominican authorities: heart attack.

I covered the story for 800 words. The American media moved on within the week. The resort issued a statement of condolences. Bahia Principe properties continued operating without modification.

But the family had hired a private investigator before the bodies were even released. And he called me.

"The girls had no medical history," he said. "There's no way both daughters had heart attacks the same night."

He sent me his report.

The room had a malfunctioning gas water heater in the bathroom. Improperly vented. Six days after the family was found, the air in that room was still measuring above 200 PPM of carbon monoxide.

Then he told me something I haven't been able to stop thinking about.

"There was a CO detector in the hallway. Residential-grade. The hotel installed it for show. The bedroom where they died — where the gas was building up overnight — didn't have one at all. And the hallway detector never made a sound."

I asked him why.

"Because it's calibrated to alarm at 70 PPM. The CO in the hallway never reached 70. It slowly poisoned everyone in the sealed bedroom, where the air didn't circulate. Standard detector. Working as designed. Nothing was technically broken."

The wife had been a nurse. The husband had been an attorney. Both daughters were in elementary school.

Their autopsy ruled "natural causes" because chronic CO poisoning is difficult to identify without specific testing for carboxyhemoglobin. Most coroners abroad don't run that test. Most American coroners don't either — not when the body comes home already labeled "heart attack."

That's the moment something broke in me.

Because that family did everything right. They booked a major chain. Top reviews. They had life insurance. They had college funds for the girls.

And they died because the detector in their hallway was calibrated for a threshold that doesn't apply to a sealed hotel bedroom with two children sleeping in it.

Not because it was defective.

Because it's working exactly the way the industry designed it to work.

Over the next six years, I documented 30 more cases.

11 in the Dominican Republic. 8 in Mexico. 4 in Costa Rica. 3 in Belize. 5 across other Caribbean and Central American destinations.

Same pattern in nearly every one. Sealed room. Gas appliance. Generator exhaust. Pool heater. Vacation rental host who installed a detector once and never replaced it.

Same coroner ruling: natural causes.

After the third case, I stopped traveling internationally without my own portable detector.

It's the kind people who actually measure this for a living — industrial CO monitoring veterans, HVAC professionals — quietly use in their own homes. Real-time digital readout instead of just a green light. Alarms long before the standard threshold. Plugs in or runs on battery. Fits in a carry-on.

I've refused to stay in three resort rooms in four years because of what it showed me.

In one case, the property sent maintenance to the room I'd vacated. The next morning, they discovered a corroded water heater vent.

I cannot prove that detector saved my family that night.

But I cannot stop thinking about it either.

If you're booking an international family trip this year, I wrote up everything below — the full pattern, the cases I've documented, and the exact setup I bring with me on every trip my kids are on.

headline: The Detector Never Went Off. The Green Light Was On the Whole Time.
body:
Nobody expects the text that changes everything.
Mom stopped responding. The nanny said the detector never went off. The green light was on the whole time.

That green light doesn't mean the air is safe. It means the unit has power. The sensor inside has a lifespan of 5 to 7 years. When it dies the light stays on. The test button still beeps. Nothing tells you it stopped working.

Standard detectors are also designed to stay silent until CO reaches 70 parts per million. By then your family has been breathing poison for hours. Headaches. Dizziness. Confusion. Not waking up when you call their name.

headline: 4,000 Homes. One Thing He Can't Tell You.
body:
I've been a home inspector for twenty-two years. Around 4,000 homes.

I can find a failing sump pump from the rust pattern on the discharge pipe. I can identify improper gas line installation from across a mechanical room. I've caught hidden water damage, undersized electrical panels, active termite activity behind finished walls.

I cannot tell you what the radon level is in your home.

That's not false modesty. A home inspection evaluates visible and accessible systems. Radon is invisible and measurable only with equipment designed to track it over time — equipment that isn't part of a standard inspection and in most states isn't something I'm licensed to test for as part of my core service.

When radon testing gets added on — and it's often optional, ordered separately, sometimes by a different contractor — what happens is a short-term charcoal canister sits in the lowest livable area for a few days. If the number comes back under 4.0 pCi/L the radon section of the transaction closes. Everyone moves forward.

I watched this happen for years and treated it the way most people in my industry do — as a process that works well enough.

Then a client called me eighteen months after I'd inspected his house. He wasn't calling to complain. He'd gotten into a conversation with a neighbor about radon and bought a continuous monitor. His winter average was 7.4 pCi/L. The inspection test had come back at 2.8.

He wasn't angry. He just wanted to understand how that was possible.

I explained what I knew. A short-term test captures a window of days. Radon in a home is not static — it moves with pressure outside, soil conditions, temperature, how tightly the house is sealed for the season. A house inspected in October in moderate conditions will often read lower than the same house measured through a sealed January with the heating system running and the ground frozen beneath the foundation. The inspection result was accurate for those days. It just wasn't a permanent description of that home.

My report tells buyers what happened once. It says nothing about what happens after they move in. Not after a renovation that seals the space further. Not after two winters of normal foundation movement. Not what the house does at its worst point of the year — which is almost never the week someone happens to be running a test canister downstairs.

I started recommending continuous monitors to buyers about two years ago. Not because it's in my inspection scope — it isn't. Because it fills the gap my report can't. A report tells you what was observed once. A monitor tells you what's happening now.

Most people find out about radon the same way.

Someone mentions it. A test comes back. They read something online. And then they realize they've been living in their house for years without ever thinking about it.

That's when the question hits.

How long has this been going on.

Not what do I do next. Not is it dangerous. The first question is almost always how long. Because radon doesn't show up one day and announce itself. It's been there. It was there last winter. It was probably there the winter before that. You just didn't have any way to know.

That's not a mistake you made. Radon has no smell. No color. Nothing your body can detect. There's no signal that something is off. Your instincts work fine — there's just nothing here for them to catch. So you go about your life assuming things are fine because nothing has ever told you otherwise.

Most people in that position have one of two things to go off. A test result from when they bought the house. Or nothing at all.

If you have a result, it's probably sitting in a folder somewhere. A number from a few days during the inspection. Under 4.0 so you moved on. What that result doesn't tell you is what your home looks like right now. Radon goes up in winter when the house seals tight. It shifts when pressure changes outside. It can be fine in September and elevated in January. That old number is accurate. It just describes a few days that are now years behind you.

If you have nothing, you're going off the fact that nothing bad has happened. Which is not the same as knowing nothing bad is happening.

Either way, you're guessing.

Here's what changes that.

A continuous monitor sits in your home and reads the air. Every hour. Every day. It shows you the actual level right now — not a snapshot from closing, not a guess, not an assumption. A number. Current. Real.

If the level is low, you know your home is fine. Not think. Not assume. Know.

If it's elevated, you find out now instead of years from now.

That's it. You plug it in. It does the work. You check the number the same way you'd check the temperature outside — quickly, without thinking much about it, because the information is just there.

People who get one almost always say the same thing. They didn't realize how much they were carrying until it was gone. The not-knowing has a weight to it. Most people don't notice it until they put it down.

The one linked below is what I'd recommend. It reads continuously, shows real-time levels, and doesn't need any setup beyond plugging it in.

You might check it and find out you're completely fine. A lot of people do.

But right now you don't know that. And there's no reason to keep not knowing.

https://truststeadfast.com/pages/clarity

headline: If you own a flat-faced dog, please read this...
body:
At 2:14 PM, Bruno was lying on the kitchen tile. At 2:31 PM, he was dead.

Seventeen minutes.

That's how fast a French Bulldog can go from seeking relief to dying on the floor his owner thought was keeping him cool.

And the worst part? The warning signs had been there for two summers. His owner just didn't know what she was looking at.

My name is Dr. Nancy Brown, DVM. I've treated over 11,000 flat-faced breed emergencies at Westfield Animal Hospital across 14 years of practice.

And I'm about to tell you about the hidden biological failure that's making every cooling product on the market dangerous for French Bulldogs, Pugs, English Bulldogs, and Boxers.

Bruno belonged to a client named Sarah. Four years old. Perfect weight. Climate controlled apartment that never went above 72 degrees.

Cooling mat in the living room. Fan pointed directly at it. Walks only before 7 AM.

Sarah had mentioned at his last checkup that Bruno kept leaving his cooling mat and going to the kitchen tile every afternoon. Like clockwork.

I told her it was probably just a preference. Some dogs like hard surfaces. It's one of the most common things I hear from flat-faced breed owners.

So she kept ignoring it.

On that Thursday in July, Sarah was working from home. Bruno was on the kitchen tile while she was on a call in the next room.

When she came out twenty minutes later he wasn't moving.

She drove to my clinic with him in her lap.

His body temperature was 107.4 degrees. Respiratory rate 68 breaths per minute. Full heat stroke.

We tried everything. Cold water immersion. IV fluids. Oxygen.

Bruno died at 3:47 PM.

I sat alone in my office that evening staring at his file. Climate controlled apartment. Cooling mat. Fan. Early morning walks only.

And I kept coming back to the same thing Sarah had told me at his last checkup.

He goes to the tile every afternoon. Just a preference.

Those were my words. My dismissal.

After Bruno I pulled every brachycephalic heat emergency case from my files going back three years and cross referenced each one with the cooling products the owner had been using.

94% of flat-faced breeds that presented with heat emergencies in my clinic were using at least one cooling product at the time.

These weren't negligent owners. These were owners who had spent money, done research, followed every recommendation I and every other vet had given them.

And their dogs were still dying.

I had been telling owners for eleven years that cooling mats and vests were adequate protection for flat-faced breeds.

I was wrong.

I spent three months pulling every study on brachycephalic thermoregulation I could find. Veterinary research on airway anatomy. Comparative studies on heat emergency presentations across breeds. Biomechanical data on respiratory effort and metabolic heat generation.

What I found contradicted everything the pet cooling industry is built on.

Flat-faced breeds begin experiencing dangerous heat stress at just 70-71 degrees Fahrenheit. Not 90 degrees like normal dogs. Seventy-one degrees.

Over 90% of French Bulldogs have measurable airway restriction significant enough to directly impair their ability to regulate temperature.

But the most important discovery wasn't in the temperature data.

It was in what happens inside a flat-faced dog's body when it tries to cool itself.

Dogs cool themselves almost entirely through panting. Approximately 70% of a dog's body heat leaves through moisture evaporating from the tongue, nasal passages, and lungs during respiration.

Panting isn't just breathing. It is their entire cooling system.

But French Bulldogs, Pugs, English Bulldogs, and Boxers have Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome — BOAS. Four simultaneous anatomical abnormalities. Stenotic nares so narrow they restrict incoming airflow. An elongated soft palate that obstructs the airway. A hypoplastic trachea congenitally undersized for the dog's body. And everted laryngeal saccules that worsen the obstruction progressively over time.

When a flat-faced dog pants harder to cool down, the muscular effort of forcing air through those compressed passages generates metabolic heat.

So the harder they pant, the more heat their body produces.

The more heat produced, the harder they pant.

The harder they pant, the hotter they get.

I call this the Broken Panting Loop.

Think of it like trying to cool your house with an air conditioner whose compressor generates more heat than the unit removes. The harder it runs the hotter the house gets. The hotter the house gets the harder it runs.

Without something breaking it from the outside, the loop accelerates toward crisis.

And here is what makes this a betrayal.

Every single cooling product currently marketed for flat-faced breeds was designed as if this loop doesn't exist.

Every mat. Every vest. Every elevated bed. Every fan.

All of them built on one catastrophically wrong assumption. That your dog's panting can finish the job.

After identifying the Broken Panting Loop I tested the most popular cooling products against brachycephalic patients in my clinic.

I tracked how long each product maintained effective cooling, what happened at the point of failure, and how the dog's respiratory rate and behavior changed throughout.

Standard gel cooling mat. The one in every French Bulldog group. Four and a half stars. Thousands of reviews.

Effective for 60-90 minutes before equilibrating to body temperature.

For a Labrador whose panting works the mat does 20% and panting does 80%. For a French Bulldog whose panting generates more heat than it releases the mat does 20% and hands off to a system making things actively worse.

After 90 minutes the dog is lying on a surface the same temperature as their own body with a fully active Broken Panting Loop and zero external support.

That's why Bruno was on the tile. Not preference. The mat had stopped working and the tile was the coldest thing left.

Cooling vest. Sold everywhere. Marketed specifically for brachycephalic breeds.

Designed to keep the coat damp so evaporative cooling reduces body temperature. Evaporative cooling requires functional panting.

For a flat-faced breed whose nasal passages are the width of two pencil lines there is no meaningful evaporative exchange happening.

Dries out within 35-45 minutes. Becomes damp fabric around a dog whose evaporative system was never going to work.

I watched owners resoak these every 40 minutes believing they were helping. They weren't.

Elevated mesh bed. Marketed to increase airflow for cooling.

The physics require functional panting. Increasing airflow around a broken evaporative system does nothing.

Portable fan directed at the dog. Moves air.

Air movement assists evaporative cooling when evaporative cooling works. For a brachycephalic breed in the Broken Panting Loop, a fan moves warm air across a dog whose panting is accelerating the crisis.

Every product. Same wrong assumption. Same failure point. Same tile.

I was so angry I couldn't sleep.

The pet cooling industry has been selling flat-faced breed owners products designed for Labradors and Golden Retrievers and putting a French Bulldog photo on the packaging.

The products work for those breeds. They were built for those breeds.

Nobody is telling you that your dog's biology is so fundamentally different that every single one of them was failing your dog from the day you bought it.

After understanding the real problem I spent two months looking for a solution built around the correct assumption.

Not assist the panting. Replace it.

The external solution has to do the cooling the body cannot complete. Not help. Replace.

I ordered every cooling product that claimed longer duration. I tested twelve additional products on brachycephalic patients using the same protocol.

Eleven of them were variations of the same gel technology. Different packaging. Different price points. Same material underneath. Same 60-90 minute failure window. Same handoff to the Broken Panting Loop.

Then I found the FureverPets CoolBreath™ Mat.

I was skeptical. I had twelve failed products and two months of dead ends behind me.

I called them. They answered immediately.

I asked for their thermal testing data. They sent it within the hour from an independent third party lab.

Sustained cooling at consistent temperature for 4-6 hours in ambient conditions of 75 degrees Fahrenheit. Not 90 minutes. Four to six hours.

I asked about the technology. Phase change material. Not gel. Not water.

A material engineered to absorb heat through a sustained molecular process. The same technology used in medical cooling devices for MS patients and athletes whose bodies cannot regulate temperature normally.

For a dog whose panting works the difference between this and a gel mat is marginal.

For a dog whose panting is driving the Broken Panting Loop, the difference is everything.
